answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

**interesting/meaningful work tends to matter a lot for sustained job satisfaction and well-being—usually more than modest differences in pay.**  
Direct research on **“regret”** is much thinner, but the closest proxies (job satisfaction, burnout, turnover intentions, career satisfaction, life satisfaction) point in the same direction.

A good way to frame your question in the literature is not “interesting” vs “uninteresting,” but:

- **intrinsic job quality**: interesting tasks, autonomy, learning, skill use, meaning
- **fit**: how well the job matches your interests/values
- **extrinsic rewards**: salary, title, prestige
- **growth/leadership opportunity**: promotion, managing people, career trajectory

## 1) Interesting work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A classic result here is the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses of work design.

### Work design / interestingness
The strongest recurring predictors of job satisfaction are things like:

- **autonomy**
- **task variety / skill variety**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to use abilities**

A widely cited meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

They integrated a large literature on work design and found that these intrinsic job features have **moderate to fairly strong associations** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout / strain

The key pattern is robust: **jobs that are more inherently engaging and meaningful are associated with substantially better day-to-day work experience.**

## 2) Meaningful work has one of the strongest relationships with satisfaction

A more recent meta-analytic literature looks specifically at **meaningful work**.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

Main findings, roughly:

- meaningful work has a **strong positive association** with **job satisfaction**
- also positive with **life satisfaction**, **work engagement**, **organizational commitment**
- negative with **turnover intentions** and **burnout**

The magnitude is not tiny. In this literature, the correlations are usually in the **moderate** range, often much larger than the typical pay–satisfaction link.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I genuinely care about the problems and enjoy the work itself,” research generally says that matters a lot.

## 3) Person-job fit also matters a lot

Another relevant line of research is **person-environment fit**, especially:

- **person-job fit**
- **person-organization fit**
- **interest congruence**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

found that fit is meaningfully related to:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit

Separate work in vocational psychology on **interest congruence** tends to find a **smaller but still positive** effect: people are more satisfied and more persistent when their jobs align with what they actually like doing.

So if one role fits your intellectual interests much better, that is not just a “nice to have” in the literature; it’s a real predictor of satisfaction and retention.

## 4) Salary matters, but usually less than people expect for satisfaction

On salary specifically, the classic finding is:

- **pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but only weakly to moderately**

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction

found a **small positive correlation** between pay level and job satisfaction—roughly in the neighborhood of **.1 to .2**, depending on specification/corrections.

That is much smaller than many intrinsic job characteristics.

Important nuances:

- **Pay matters more when pay is low or financial stress is real**
- **Pay fairness** often matters more than absolute pay
- **Relative pay / social comparison** matters a lot
- Beyond a comfortable income, extra money still helps on average, but the **marginal psychological return is smaller**

In your case, if the offer is “a bit higher salary” rather than life-changing money, the literature would generally not predict that alone to dominate day-to-day well-being.

## 5) Promotion / leading a team: positive for some, but not universally

This part is more mixed.

Research on **promotions** and **managerial roles** generally suggests:

- promotions often produce a **short-term bump** in satisfaction
- the effect often **partly fades** over time
- the net effect depends a lot on whether the role brings:
  - more autonomy and growth
  - or more stress, conflict, admin burden, and role overload

This matters because “leading a team” is not purely extrinsic. If it’s something you’ve **always wanted**, it may function more like an **intrinsic/self-concordant goal** than like a title bump.

That distinction matters in the literature.

## 6) Self-concordant goals: wanting leadership can matter a lot

General motivation research, especially **Self-Determination Theory** and **self-concordance** work, suggests that people do better when pursuing goals that feel genuinely their own.

Relevant themes:

- **autonomous motivation** predicts better well-being and engagement than controlled/extrinsic motivation
- **self-concordant goals**—goals you endorse deeply, rather than pursue for status or pressure—are linked to better well-being when attained

So if “leading a small team” is something you authentically want, research would not treat that as just a prestige factor. It may carry some of the same well-being weight as “interesting work,” because it supports:

- autonomy
- competence
- growth
- identity

That’s one reason this isn’t a simple “interesting work beats money” story.  
A wanted leadership opportunity may be psychologically much more important than a generic promotion.

## 7) On regret specifically: not much direct evidence

I don’t know of a strong, central literature asking:

> “Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid less interesting one?”

But the closest proxies are fairly consistent:

People in roles with higher intrinsic interest / meaning / fit tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher career satisfaction
- lower desire to leave
- lower burnout
- better overall well-being

That’s the best empirical answer available.

One caveat: there is a smaller literature suggesting meaningful work can have a **dark side**:

- people tolerate worse conditions for work they care about
- they may overwork or accept exploitation
- “mission-driven” work can increase self-sacrifice

So interesting work is not a free lunch if it also brings chronic overwork or poor boundaries.

## 8) Rough ranking from the literature

If I compress the evidence a lot, the pattern is something like:

### Strong predictors of sustained job satisfaction
- meaningfulness
- autonomy
- interesting/challenging work
- skill use and learning
- person-job/value fit

### Smaller predictors
- pay level by itself

### Mixed / highly conditional
- promotion / leadership
- prestige
- title

That doesn’t mean pay and leadership don’t matter. It means that **for ongoing happiness at work**, intrinsic characteristics usually have stronger and more reliable links.

## 9) What the research would imply for a case like yours

Without giving personal advice, the literature would say something like:

1. **If the salary increase is modest**, it is unlikely by itself to offset a large drop in interest/meaning.
2. **If the new role gives you a genuinely desired leadership opportunity**, that may matter much more than “more money,” because it could satisfy deep motives for growth and autonomy.
3. **If the new work is substantially less interesting**, the best-supported prediction is lower day-to-day job satisfaction unless the leadership aspect compensates strongly.
4. Direct “regret” evidence is limited, but the proxy measures suggest that **staying with more interesting/fitting work generally predicts better well-being than choosing primarily for pay**.

## 10) The most relevant papers to look up

If you want a short reading list, I’d start with:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
  *Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.*

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
  *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*

- **Judge et al. (2010)**  
  *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.*

If you want, I can also do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more rigorous mini literature review** with 10–15 papers and effect sizes, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the research variables** rather than opinion.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**Jobs that people experience as interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and a good fit tend to produce higher job satisfaction and better well-being than jobs that are better only on extrinsic dimensions like pay or status.**  
But there are two important caveats:

1. **“Interesting work” is not usually studied under that exact label.** Researchers usually break it into things like:
   - task variety / challenge
   - autonomy
   - opportunities to learn and use skills
   - meaningfulness / task significance
   - person–job fit

2. **Direct “did they regret choosing the more interesting job?” evidence is sparse.** The literature is much stronger on satisfaction, engagement, burnout, turnover, and well-being than on explicit retrospective regret.

## What the strongest findings say

### 1) Interesting/meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
A large work-design literature going back to **Hackman & Oldham** found that jobs with:
- more skill variety
- more autonomy
- clearer task identity
- greater task significance
- better feedback

produce more internal motivation and satisfaction.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Finding: “motivational” job characteristics such as **autonomy, variety, and significance** had substantial positive associations with **job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement**, and negative associations with withdrawal/turnover-related outcomes.

In practical terms: the literature treats “interesting work” as a very consequential job attribute, not a nice-to-have.

### 2) Meaningful work predicts not just job satisfaction, but broader well-being
A later meta-analysis focused specifically on **meaningful work**:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, *Journal of Management Studies*  
  Finding: meaningful work was positively associated with **job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction**, and negatively associated with **burnout** and **turnover intentions**.

So if by “interesting” you mean “this work genuinely engages me and feels worth doing,” that is associated not only with liking the job more, but with better general well-being.

### 3) Intrinsic motivation tends to beat extrinsic motivation for well-being
A large literature in **Self-Determination Theory** finds that work is better for people when it supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

This is relevant because interesting work often supports autonomy/competence more than “better-paid but less engaging” work.

Good review:

- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**, *Annual Review of Organizational Psychology and Organizational Behavior*  
  Finding: more autonomous/intrinsic motivation is associated with **better performance, persistence, and psychological well-being**; more controlled/extrinsic motivation is generally weaker for well-being.

This does **not** mean money/status don’t matter. It means that for happiness and satisfaction, intrinsic qualities of work are usually more potent.

### 4) Pay matters, but usually less than people think for job satisfaction
There is a robust finding that **pay level has a positive but modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  Finding: the relationship between **how much people are paid** and **overall job satisfaction** is positive, but **not especially large**.

This is one of the most relevant findings for your case. A “bit higher salary” usually does help somewhat, but the literature does **not** suggest that modest pay gains are as important as daily job experience.

At the broader life-satisfaction level, income still matters, but:
- the effect is usually **diminishing** with higher income,
- and at already high professional income levels, **job quality differences can dominate day-to-day experience**.

### 5) Person–job fit is very important
If one job fits your interests, strengths, and identity better, that matters a lot.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis on person–job and person–organization fit.  
  Finding: better fit is associated with **higher job satisfaction and commitment**, and lower intentions to quit.

This is important because the research would not ask only:
> “Interesting vs higher salary?”

It would also ask:
> “Which role is a better fit for how you actually like to spend your working hours?”

## What about regret?
This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research that directly asks:
> “People who chose the more interesting job over the better-paid/promoted one—do they later regret it?”

What the indirect evidence suggests is:

- People who emphasize **intrinsic work values** tend to report **better well-being** than those emphasizing mainly **extrinsic rewards**.
- People in jobs experienced as meaningful/interesting tend to show **lower turnover intentions** and **less burnout**.
- Higher pay and promotions often yield gains, but people **adapt** to some extrinsic improvements faster than to the recurring daily experience of the work itself.

So the evidence is **more supportive of “people are happier when work is interesting/meaningful”** than of “people regret choosing that.”  
I don’t know of good evidence showing a general pattern of **systematic regret** from prioritizing interesting work, assuming pay/security remain strong.

## Important nuance for your exact situation
Your choice is not just:

- interesting work vs more money

It is also:

- individual contributor work vs leading a small team

And leadership itself is an intrinsically meaningful attribute for many people:
- more autonomy
- more responsibility
- greater impact
- identity growth
- development of a long-term desired skill

So from a research perspective, the Anthropic option may be **less interesting in content**, but possibly **more interesting in role design**.

That matters because the literature would treat “I always wanted to lead a team” as a real source of intrinsic motivation, not just a status move.

## Best-supported summary
If I compress the literature into a few evidence-based takeaways:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
2. **The association is usually stronger than the effect of modest differences in pay.**
3. **Person–job fit matters a lot.**
4. **Direct evidence on later regret is limited, but the broader evidence does not suggest that choosing intrinsically rewarding work is a common mistake.**
5. **Leadership opportunities complicate the picture, because they may themselves satisfy intrinsic motives for growth, autonomy, and impact.**

## If you want a few concrete papers to read
A short reading list:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary. *Journal of Applied Psychology*.  
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis. *Journal of Management Studies*.  
- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M. (2017).** Self-determination theory in work organizations. *Annual Review of Organizational Psychology and Organizational Behavior*.  
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis. *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis. *Personnel Psychology*.

If you want, I can also turn this into a **very compact evidence table** specifically for your decision, e.g.:
- interestingness of content
- leadership growth
- autonomy
- meaning
- pay
- fit
- burnout risk
with “what the literature predicts” for each.

answer:
Yes. The research literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**Once pay is already “good enough,” the intrinsic quality of the work tends to matter more for job satisfaction and well-being than modest differences in salary.**

But there’s an important nuance in your case: **“leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsic good** if leadership is something you deeply want, not just a status marker.

## What the literature usually means by “interesting work”
Researchers rarely use “interesting” as a single variable. They break it into things like:

- **Intrinsic motivation / enjoyment**
- **Skill variety / challenge**
- **Autonomy**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Learning / growth**
- **Fit with personal values and identity**

That matters because a job can be “less intellectually interesting” but still higher on autonomy, impact, or identity fit.

---

## 1) Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A large work-design literature finds that jobs with more:

- autonomy
- variety
- significance
- feedback
- opportunities to use skill

produce higher **job satisfaction**, **internal motivation**, and often better performance.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of work design. Their overall result: **motivational job characteristics** such as autonomy, variety, and task significance are reliably associated with higher satisfaction and motivation.

This is one of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology.

### Interpretation for your question
If “staying” means continuing to do work that is substantially more engaging day-to-day, the literature would generally predict **higher job satisfaction** from that, all else equal.

---

## 2) Meaningful work predicts not just job satisfaction, but broader well-being

There is also a strong literature on **meaningful work**, which overlaps a lot with “interesting and worth doing.”

Meta-analytic evidence suggests meaningful work is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- higher life satisfaction / psychological well-being
- lower turnover intentions
- lower burnout / distress

A useful reference:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes

The basic pattern is robust: people who experience their work as meaningful tend to do better psychologically.

### Interpretation
If your current role feels materially more meaningful or intellectually alive, that isn’t a trivial preference. Research treats it as a major predictor of well-being.

---

## 3) Pay matters — but usually less than people expect for ongoing satisfaction

There is solid evidence that **pay is positively related to job satisfaction**, but the relationship is **modest**, not dominant.

A key source:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

Their conclusion, in plain English:  
**money matters, but not nearly as much as many people assume when predicting long-term satisfaction.**

Also, pay tends to matter more through:

- perceived fairness
- social comparison
- whether basic financial stress is reduced

than through “a bit more money” once someone is already highly compensated.

### Interpretation
If the Anthropic offer is only **a bit higher salary**, the literature would not usually predict that alone to outweigh a large drop in interest/meaning.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motives predict well-being better than extrinsic motives

This comes from **self-determination theory** and adjacent research.

The broad result is:

- pursuing **intrinsic goals** (growth, mastery, contribution, relationships, meaningful activity) is associated with better well-being
- prioritizing **extrinsic goals** (money, status, image) is less reliably associated with well-being, and sometimes negatively so when it crowds out intrinsic motives

Useful references:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**, work motivation review
- **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**, self-determination theory in work organizations
- **Kasser & Ryan (1993, 1996)**, intrinsic vs extrinsic goals and well-being

### Interpretation
If the move is primarily:
- more money
- more status
- more impressive title

but clearly less interesting day-to-day, the literature is generally skeptical that this improves happiness much.

If, however, the move satisfies a strong intrinsic motive — e.g. **“I’ve always wanted to lead people”** — then it is not just an extrinsic move. It may be intrinsically valuable.

---

## 5) Fit matters a lot: the best predictor is often not “interesting vs salary,” but “which role fits who you are”

The **person-job fit / person-organization fit** literature shows that when jobs align better with a person’s interests, values, and identity, people tend to have:

- higher job satisfaction
- stronger commitment
- lower turnover intentions

Classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis on person-environment fit

This is especially relevant for your case because you are comparing two different identities:

1. **researcher doing unusually interesting work**
2. **leader/manager of a small team**

If leading has been a serious long-term aspiration, that can be a major positive through **identity fit**, even if the domain is less stimulating.

### Interpretation
Research would say: don’t compare only “interesting work” vs “more money.”  
Compare:

- current role’s fit with your identity as a researcher
- new role’s fit with your identity as a leader

Whichever is more central to you tends to predict satisfaction better.

---

## 6) On regret specifically: direct evidence is weaker

This part is less clean.

I don’t know of a strong body of research showing, specifically:

> people who choose the more interesting job systematically regret it less than those who choose the higher-paying less interesting one

That exact comparison is not well studied in a definitive way.

What we do have:

### A. Misfit predicts dissatisfaction and turnover
If people take roles that fit their interests/values poorly, they are more likely to become dissatisfied and want to leave.

### B. General regret research suggests long-term regret often centers on opportunities not taken
Classic work by:

- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**

finds that over the long term, people often regret **inactions** more than actions — i.e., roads not taken.

But that’s a broad regret result, not specific to careers, and it cuts both ways in your case:
- you might regret **not taking** the leadership opportunity
- or regret **leaving** uniquely interesting research

So I would treat regret evidence as suggestive, not decisive.

---

## What the literature would probably say about your specific tradeoff

Given the facts you gave:

- current job: **very interesting**
- new job: **higher pay**
- new job: **lead a small team**
- new job: **much less interesting**

A research-based summary would be:

### If the salary increase is modest and your current compensation is already high:
The literature would usually predict **more happiness from staying with the more intrinsically rewarding work** than from chasing the pay bump.

### But if leading a team is a deeply held goal:
Then the new role may not actually be “less intrinsically rewarding overall.”  
It may be:
- less interesting intellectually
- but more meaningful in terms of growth, identity, and agency

In that case the research on **person-job fit** and **intrinsic motivation** says the leadership role could plausibly be better for satisfaction.

So the decisive question from the literature is not:

> “Is interesting work more important than money?”

It is more like:

> “Which option better satisfies my intrinsic motives: deep research interest, or the desire to lead/build?”

---

## If you want the most evidence-based bottom line

Here’s the shortest faithful summary:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work** is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
- **Pay** matters, but usually less than people forecast, especially when already well paid.
- **Fit with identity and values** matters a lot.
- **Direct regret evidence is limited**, but poor intrinsic fit is a known risk factor for later dissatisfaction.

So if the Anthropic role is mainly:
- a bit more money
- a title bump
- less interesting work

then the literature leans toward **staying**.

If it is mainly:
- a chance to do something you intrinsically care about a lot — **leading and building**
- with enough autonomy and impact to compensate for lower intellectual interest

then the literature does **not** clearly favor staying.

---

## Good papers to start with

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Journal of Applied Psychology.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Journal of Vocational Behavior.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   Personnel Psychology.

4. **Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
   *Self-determination theory and work motivation.*  
   Journal of Organizational Behavior.

5. **Deci, Olafsen, & Ryan (2017)**  
   *Self-determination theory in work organizations: The state of a science.*  
   Annual Review of Organizational Psychology and Organizational Behavior.

6. **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   Meta-analytic work on meaningful work and well-being outcomes.

7. **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)**  
   *The experience of regret: What, when, and why.*  
   Psychological Review.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision framework based only on empirically supported predictors** — e.g. a 6-factor scorecard using intrinsic motivation, autonomy, meaning, leadership fit, compensation delta, and regret risk.

answer:
Yes — there’s a fairly strong research literature suggesting that **intrinsically rewarding work** (interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich, growth-oriented) is a major predictor of job satisfaction and broader well-being, often **more strongly than pay** once compensation is already “good enough.”

The short version of the literature is:

1. **Interesting work matters a lot for happiness at work.**
2. **Pay matters, but less than many people think for satisfaction, especially at higher income levels.**
3. **What tends to matter most is not “interestingness” alone, but a cluster:**  
   **autonomy + skill use/growth + meaning + fit with one’s values/identity.**
4. **There is much less direct research on regret** from this exact kind of career choice, but adjacent evidence suggests that choices aligned with intrinsic interests and values tend to produce better long-run satisfaction than choices driven mainly by extrinsic rewards.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly tied to job satisfaction

A classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses on **work design**.

The core findings are that people tend to be more satisfied and motivated when their work has:

- **skill variety**  
- **task identity**  
- **task significance**  
- **autonomy**  
- **feedback**

Those are basically the ingredients of what many people informally mean by “interesting work.”

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: work design characteristics such as autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback showed substantial positive associations with **job satisfaction**, **intrinsic motivation**, and related outcomes.

This is one of the most robust findings in organizational psychology: jobs that are more engaging and growth-rich are associated with higher satisfaction and lower burnout risk.

## 2) Pay has a weaker relationship with job satisfaction than people often assume

There is also a well-known finding that **pay level and job satisfaction are only weakly related**.

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** found the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** to be relatively small (roughly in the **.1–.2 range**, depending on how it’s estimated).

That does **not** mean pay is irrelevant. It means:

- pay matters a lot when it affects security, stress, or perceived fairness
- but once those are in place, **more money buys much less additional satisfaction** than many people expect

Also, research often finds that **pay fairness** and **relative pay** matter more psychologically than absolute salary.

So if your choice is between:
- “more interesting work, slightly less pay”
- versus “less interesting work, slightly more pay”

the literature generally says that the **interestingness loss is psychologically more consequential** than the modest salary gain, assuming both salaries are already comfortably high.

## 3) Meaningful/intrinsically motivated work predicts well-being

A related literature looks at **meaningful work** and **autonomous motivation**.

Findings from **self-determination theory** and related work show that people do better when work satisfies basic psychological needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Research reviews and meta-analytic work generally find that when those needs are met at work, people report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- better mental well-being
- lower burnout and turnover intentions

Relevant names here include:

- **Deci & Ryan** / **Gagné & Deci**
- meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work, e.g. **Van den Broeck** and colleagues
- meta-analyses on meaningful work, e.g. **Allan** and colleagues

The broad pattern is very consistent:  
**intrinsic quality of work** predicts better outcomes than purely extrinsic features like pay or status.

## 4) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

This matters because your question is really about being “happier,” not just liking the job more.

There’s a long literature showing that **job satisfaction and life satisfaction are moderately correlated** and likely influence each other over time.

Classic estimates are around:

- **r ≈ .4 to .45** between job satisfaction and life satisfaction

That’s not perfect overlap, but it’s substantial. So when researchers say interesting, autonomous, meaningful work predicts job satisfaction, that usually has implications for broader well-being too.

In other words: staying in a more interesting role doesn’t just tend to make people like the job more; it often contributes to overall life satisfaction as well.

## 5) People often accept lower pay for more interesting/meaningful work

Economists approach this via **compensating differentials**: if a job has attractive non-monetary features, employers can often pay less because workers value those features.

There’s evidence that people are willing to trade money for things like:

- meaningful work
- autonomy
- flexibility
- mission alignment
- intellectually engaging work

A classic example relevant to research careers:

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found evidence that scientists accept lower wages for the opportunity to do scientific work itself.

That doesn’t prove they’re happier in every case, but it does show that many workers **reveal** a strong preference for interesting work over higher pay.

## 6) Intrinsic goals tend to predict better well-being than extrinsic goals

Another adjacent literature compares **intrinsic** aspirations (growth, relationships, community, meaningful contribution) with **extrinsic** aspirations (money, image, status).

A lot of this work, associated with **Kasser & Ryan**, finds that stronger emphasis on extrinsic goals is often linked to **lower well-being**, while intrinsic goals are linked to **better well-being**.

Applied cautiously to careers, this suggests:

- choosing based mainly on money/status tends to be less satisfying long-term
- choosing based on interest, growth, contribution, and fit tends to be more satisfying

## 7) What about regret?

This is the part where the evidence is thinner.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research specifically asking:

> “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job rather than taking a somewhat better-paid leadership role?”

That exact counterfactual is hard to study.

What the adjacent literature suggests is:

- people do better long-term when choices align with their **intrinsic interests**, **values**, and **identity**
- people tend to do worse when they move toward roles that are “better on paper” but a poorer fit psychologically
- **person-job fit** and **person-vocation fit** are reliable predictors of satisfaction and retention

So on regret, the best-supported statement is probably:

> Research does not give a crisp answer on regret for this exact decision, but it strongly suggests that **value-congruent, intrinsically satisfying work choices** are less likely to produce long-run dissatisfaction than choices driven mainly by extrinsic gains.

## 8) One important complication in your case: leadership itself may be an intrinsic good

Your situation has an important twist: the new role is not just “more money, less interesting work.” It also offers **leading a small team**, which you say is something you’ve always wanted.

From a research perspective, that matters because leadership can satisfy intrinsic needs too:

- autonomy
- impact
- competence
- identity fulfillment
- growth

So the real tradeoff is not simply:

- interest vs money

It may be:

- **topic/technical interest**
vs
- **leadership aspiration / growth / impact**

The literature would treat both as psychologically meaningful.

This is why research on **person-environment fit** is relevant: a role can be less interesting in subject matter but still be highly satisfying if it better matches an important aspiration.

## 9) What research would most likely predict in a case like yours

If I translate the literature into predictions, without giving personal advice:

### Research would predict higher long-run satisfaction from the option that gives you more of:
- autonomy
- skill use and growth
- meaning/purpose
- identity fit
- intrinsic motivation

### Research would predict smaller effects from:
- modest pay differences
- prestige differences, unless they strongly affect identity or opportunities

### Research would be cautious if the new role:
- reduces day-to-day intrinsic interest a lot
- increases stress/coordination load substantially
- moves you away from the type of work that energizes you

### Research would be more favorable to the new role if:
- leading people is a genuine long-standing aspiration
- the role increases autonomy and perceived impact
- you would find management/mentoring intrinsically rewarding, not just résumé-enhancing

## 10) If you want the most defensible summary sentence

A fair evidence-based summary would be:

> **Yes: a large body of research suggests that doing work that is interesting, meaningful, and autonomy-rich is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and broader well-being, and generally matters more for long-term satisfaction than modest differences in pay.**  
> **But the strongest predictor is fit with your intrinsic motives and values — so if leading a team is itself a deeply meaningful aspiration, that can offset a loss in topic-level interest.**

## Useful references to look up

If you want to read the actual literature, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.
- **Judge, T. A. et al. (2010).** Meta-analytic work on pay and job satisfaction.
- **Judge, T. A., & Watanabe, S. (1993).** Another look at the job satisfaction–life satisfaction relationship.
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).** Self-determination theory and work motivation.
- **Van den Broeck** and colleagues on psychological need satisfaction at work.
- **Allan** and colleagues on meaningful work and well-being.
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M.** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations.
- **Stern, S. (2004).** Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?

If you want, I can also do one of two things:

1. give you a **more formal literature-summary with 10–15 papers and one-line findings**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework explicitly grounded in the research constructs** (autonomy, competence, meaning, fit, etc.).

answer:
Yes. The strongest relevant literature is in organizational psychology and labor economics, though it usually studies **intrinsic job quality** rather than “interestingness” per se.

## Short answer

Across many studies, people tend to be **more satisfied and better off in jobs that are intrinsically rewarding** — meaning work that is interesting, autonomous, varied, meaningful, and fits their strengths/values.

By contrast, **absolute pay has a positive but usually much smaller relationship** with job satisfaction once pay is already adequate and perceived as fair.

So, if your choice is roughly:

- **more interesting work**
vs
- **slightly more money**

the literature generally points toward **interesting/intrinsic work mattering more for long-run job satisfaction**.

But your case is not just that, because the Anthropic role also offers **leadership**, and leadership can increase other powerful drivers of well-being: autonomy, competence, impact, growth, status.

So the research-based framing is probably not “interesting vs salary,” but:

- **technical/research interest**
vs
- **leadership/growth/autonomy/impact**
with salary as a secondary factor unless the pay gap is large.

---

## What the research tends to show

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A classic result is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** argued that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:
  - skill variety
  - task identity
  - task significance
  - autonomy
  - feedback

This has held up fairly well.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that these kinds of job characteristics are **substantially related** to:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - performance
  - lower strain

If by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, challenging, varied, and meaningful, that literature is directly relevant.

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

A separate literature on **meaningful work** finds that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher work engagement
- more life satisfaction
- less psychological distress
- lower turnover intentions

Meta-analytic work here includes:

- **Allan et al. (2019/2020)** on outcomes of meaningful work

The important point is: the association is not tiny. It’s typically **moderate** and often larger than the relationship between pay level and satisfaction.

### 3. Pay matters, but less than people often assume

The best-known result here is that **pay level and job satisfaction are related, but only weakly**.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis found a **small positive relationship** between pay level and job satisfaction — roughly in the “small effect” range, not a dominant one.

That doesn’t mean salary is irrelevant. It matters more when:

- pay is low relative to needs
- pay feels unfair
- pay is a signal of status/recognition
- the difference is large, not marginal
- the role changes long-run earning trajectory

But if the offer is just “a bit higher salary,” research would generally predict that this matters **less** than losing a lot of intrinsic interest.

### 4. Person-job fit matters a lot

Another strong literature is on **fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis showed that person-job fit and person-organization fit are strongly associated with:
  - job satisfaction
  - commitment
  - lower intent to quit

This matters because “interestingness” is partly objective and partly about **fit**. A job that is only moderately interesting on paper can be highly satisfying if it matches what you value most.

So if you are unusually motivated by frontier research, novelty, technical challenge, or mission, then the loss from moving to less interesting work may be **larger for you than for the average employee**.

### 5. Autonomy, competence, and growth are also major drivers

Self-determination theory is very relevant here.

That literature finds that well-being at work depends strongly on whether the role supports:

- **autonomy** — having control/agency
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — connection/respect/team belonging

Reviews by **Deci, Ryan**, and others, and organizational applications such as **Van den Broeck et al.**, consistently show that when these needs are met, people tend to have better motivation and well-being.

This is the strongest research-based argument **for** the Anthropic option:

- leading a small team may increase
  - autonomy
  - competence
  - impact
  - growth
  - recognition

So even if the work is less interesting technically, it may score better on other intrinsic dimensions.

---

## Do people who choose the more interesting job end up happier?

**On average, yes — if “interesting” means intrinsically rewarding and well-matched to them.**

That is what the literature would predict.

But a few important qualifiers:

### A. Most of this evidence is not a clean randomized experiment
People select into jobs, and happier/more capable people may also land better jobs. So the literature is strongest on:

- correlations
- longitudinal panel evidence
- job redesign studies
- meta-analytic consistency

That said, the pattern is very robust.

### B. Interesting work does not compensate for everything
People can be deeply interested and still be unhappy if the job has:

- chronic overload
- bad management
- political conflict
- low control
- low recognition
- poor work-life boundaries

There is also research on **obsessive passion**: highly engaging work can become unhealthy if it consumes identity and boundaries.

So “interesting” is very important, but not enough by itself.

### C. Leadership may satisfy different needs than technical research
A role can be less technically interesting but more satisfying overall if it offers:

- more influence
- clearer impact
- better fit with long-term identity
- more growth
- more autonomy

That’s why the empirical framing is multidimensional.

---

## What about regret specifically?

There is **much less direct research on “later regret”** in this exact sense.

The literature usually measures proxies such as:

- job satisfaction
- burnout
- engagement
- intent to quit
- actual turnover
- mental well-being

On those outcomes, the evidence generally suggests:

- jobs with better intrinsic qualities lead to **better outcomes**
- low-interest/low-fit jobs lead to **more disengagement and more desire to leave**
- higher pay does **not reliably erase** those effects

So while “regret” is not commonly measured directly, the nearest available evidence points in the same direction: **people tend to do worse in lower-interest, lower-fit work unless the move brings meaningful gains on other intrinsic dimensions**.

---

## A useful way to map your decision to the research

Your decision seems to involve at least 5 empirically relevant factors:

1. **Interest / intellectual stimulation**
2. **Meaning / mission**
3. **Autonomy / control**
4. **Growth / leadership development**
5. **Pay / status / fairness**

Research suggests that for durable satisfaction, the big ones are usually **1–4**, while **5** matters but often less unless it is large or tied to fairness/security.

So the key research question is not “interesting or more money?”  
It is closer to:

> Will the new role reduce my day-to-day intrinsic motivation more than it increases my autonomy, competence, leadership growth, and impact?

That is much closer to how the evidence is organized.

---

## If you want the most relevant papers to read

A concise reading list would be:

1. **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model  
   Foundational theory: interesting, autonomous, meaningful work drives motivation and satisfaction.

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
   Strong evidence that motivational job characteristics predict satisfaction and motivation.

3. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction meta-analysis  
   Pay matters, but its relationship with overall job satisfaction is relatively small.

4. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — person-environment fit meta-analysis  
   Strong links between fit and satisfaction/commitment.

5. **Allan et al. (2019/2020)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work  
   Meaningful work predicts satisfaction, engagement, and better well-being.

6. **Deci & Ryan / Van den Broeck et al.** — self-determination theory at work  
   Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are major drivers of work motivation and well-being.

7. **Tett & Meyer (1993)** and later turnover meta-analyses  
   Lower satisfaction predicts higher intent to quit and actual turnover.

---

## Bottom line from the research

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

**People are usually happier in jobs that are intrinsically rewarding and fit them well, and a modest salary increase often does not compensate for losing that — but leadership/growth/autonomy can be just as important as “interestingness,” so your decision is really between two different bundles of intrinsic rewards.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **more decision-oriented evidence summary** — for example, a 2x2 comparing:
- interesting work
- leadership opportunity
- salary
- long-term career capital

using only research-backed factors.

answer:
Yes. The closest research doesn’t usually compare “OpenAI-style interesting IC role” vs “slightly higher-paid, less interesting team-lead role” head-to-head, but several literatures speak to it pretty directly.

## Short version

The research is pretty consistent on three points:

1. **Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is much weaker than most people assume, especially once pay is already high/comfortable.**
3. **Promotion into management tends to help only if it fits what you actually want to do day-to-day; the average benefit is mixed and often not huge or not durable.**

So if your question is specifically **“do people who stay in more interesting work tend to be happier?”**, the answer from the literature is broadly **yes**—on average, interesting/meaningful work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction and often with broader well-being.  
If the question is **“do people regret that later?”**, there is much less direct research on regret specifically, but the evidence on **adaptation** suggests people often overestimate the lasting happiness impact of salary/status changes and underestimate the importance of what they do every day.

---

## 1) Interesting work: strong and robust predictor of satisfaction

A classic result in organizational psychology is that certain job features reliably increase intrinsic motivation and satisfaction:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

This is the **Job Characteristics Model** from **Hackman & Oldham (1976)**. The basic finding is that work feels better when it is more interesting, more whole, more significant, and gives you autonomy and visible results.

Later meta-analytic work strengthened this:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed work-design research and found that these “motivational” job characteristics are **substantially related** to:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal work motivation
  - engagement
  - lower turnover intentions

This is probably the most directly relevant literature to your phrase “interesting work.”

### Practical interpretation
If one job is clearly more intellectually engaging and intrinsically motivating than the other, the literature would predict:
- higher day-to-day satisfaction
- greater engagement
- less desire to leave
- often better overall well-being

---

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A related literature looks at **meaningful work**, which overlaps a lot with “interesting” but is not identical.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** conducted a meta-analysis on meaningful work and found that meaningful work is positively associated with:
  - **job satisfaction**
  - **work engagement**
  - **life satisfaction**
  - **organizational commitment**
  
  and negatively associated with:
  - **turnover intentions**
  - **negative affect / strain**

So if the current role feels more meaningful/interesting, the literature would generally predict it to be better for subjective well-being than a role that is merely better paid or higher status.

### Caveat
Meaningful work is not a magic shield. Some “calling” research suggests that people in meaningful jobs can also tolerate poor conditions longer than they should, or burn out if the work is absorbing but unsustainable. So “interesting” helps a lot, but not if it comes with chronic overload or bad culture.

---

## 3) Pay matters less than people usually think for job satisfaction

There is strong evidence that **pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction**.

A key meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay level and job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**.

This is one of the most useful findings for your situation. People often assume salary differences should dominate career choices, but empirically salary explains **much less** of job satisfaction than intrinsic job features do.

A few nuances from the broader literature:

- **Pay fairness** often matters more than raw pay.
- **Relative pay** matters more than people wish it did.
- Once compensation is already at a high/comfortable level, additional pay usually has **diminishing returns** for happiness.

So a “bit higher salary” is definitely not irrelevant—but the evidence suggests it is usually **not** the strongest driver of whether you’ll actually like the job.

---

## 4) Autonomy, competence, and growth matter a lot

Another very relevant framework is **Self-Determination Theory**.

At work, well-being is higher when jobs satisfy needs for:
- **autonomy** — having control over how you work
- **competence** — feeling effective and challenged
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Reviews/meta-analyses in this tradition consistently show that these needs predict:
- higher job satisfaction
- lower burnout
- better motivation
- better mental health

For your decision, this matters because “interesting research” often scores high on **competence** and often on **autonomy**, while a first-line leadership role may score high on **relatedness/influence** but lower on day-to-day competence enjoyment if it pulls you away from the actual work you like.

---

## 5) What about moving into management?

This part of the literature is much less clean.

### What seems true
Promotions often produce:
- a **short-term increase** in satisfaction
- gains in status, pay, and perceived success

But the effects are often:
- **heterogeneous**
- partly due to the wage increase or status signal
- sometimes **temporary**

One useful paper here is:

- **Kosteas (2011)**: promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, but the effect is not simple and is tied up with pay growth and expectations.

### Why management is tricky
The literature on promotion doesn’t always distinguish between “promotion to more of what you love” and “promotion away from what you love.”

For highly technical people, management can mean:
- fewer hours doing the intrinsically rewarding work
- more coordination, hiring, planning, conflict resolution, performance management
- more ambiguity and people stress

If someone genuinely wants that, satisfaction can rise. If they mostly want the idea of leadership/status, but not the daily activity, satisfaction can drop.

So the best research-based statement here is:

> **Leadership roles are not universally better for well-being; the benefit depends heavily on person-role fit.**

This is consistent with the broader **person-job fit** and **person-environment fit** literature: people are happier when the job matches their motives, strengths, and values.

---

## 6) Do people later regret choosing the more interesting work?

This exact question is not especially well studied in a clean way. I wouldn’t claim there is strong direct evidence saying “people who choose interesting jobs regret it less.”

But there are two adjacent findings that matter:

### A. People adapt to income/status gains
Research on **hedonic adaptation** and **affective forecasting** suggests that people often overestimate how much a salary/status upgrade will improve long-run happiness.

Classic work by **Gilbert, Wilson**, and others shows that people are bad at predicting how long emotional effects will last. Raises, titles, prestige shifts, etc. often feel important in advance but have less durable impact than expected.

### B. Daily experience matters a lot
If you spend most waking hours doing work that is more interesting, more engaging, and more aligned with your strengths, that tends to matter a lot for experienced well-being.

So while I’d be cautious claiming “less regret” as a directly established result, the available evidence suggests people often **underweight the quality of their everyday work experience** relative to salary/status changes.

---

## 7) If I had to summarize the evidence in your exact tradeoff

For a choice between:

- **Job A:** more interesting research, less leadership, slightly lower pay
- **Job B:** less interesting work, slightly higher pay, chance to lead a small team

the literature would say:

### More likely to improve happiness/job satisfaction:
- the job that is more **interesting**
- more **meaningful**
- more **autonomous**
- better aligned with your intrinsic motives

### More likely to matter less than you intuit:
- a **modest** salary increase

### Could matter a lot, but only if it fits:
- leading a team

So the key uncertainty is not “salary vs interest” — research is pretty favorable to **interest/meaning** there.  
The key uncertainty is whether **leading a small team is itself one of the things that makes work more meaningful for you**, enough to offset the loss in interesting content.

---

## 8) A careful evidence-based takeaway

If you want the most defensible research-based conclusion, it’s probably this:

> **On average, people are happier in work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, and autonomy-supportive than in work that is merely better paid.**
>
> **Promotion to leadership improves satisfaction mainly when the new role fits the person’s motivations and identity; otherwise it can be neutral or negative despite higher pay/status.**

That is, broadly, what the literature supports.

---

## 9) A few papers/results worth looking up

If you want to read actual sources, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model  
  Foundational theory: interesting/autonomous/significant jobs increase motivation and satisfaction.

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
  Strong evidence that motivational job characteristics predict job satisfaction and related outcomes.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction  
  Pay matters, but the relationship is modest.

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work  
  Meaningful work strongly linked to job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction.

- **Kosteas (2011)** — promotions and job satisfaction  
  Promotions help on average, but effects are not simple or uniformly large.

- Work on **Self-Determination Theory** at work  
  Autonomy, competence, relatedness are robust predictors of well-being.

- Work on **affective forecasting / impact bias** by **Gilbert & Wilson**  
  Relevant for not overweighting salary/status gains.

---

If you want, I can also do one of two things:
1. turn this into a **1-page evidence summary with citations**, or  
2. help you build a **decision matrix weighted by what the literature says predicts long-run satisfaction**.

Jebb found that satisfaction plateaus around $60-95k. Therefore, a slight salary increase likely impacts well-being less than improvements in day-to-day work quality.
answer:
Yes. The literature is fairly consistent on one broad point:

**Work that is intrinsically engaging/interesting/meaningful is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and sustained well-being, and it usually predicts these outcomes more strongly than pay level does.**

The important caveat is that “interesting” is not a single variable in research. It usually gets captured through things like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **skill variety / challenge / learning**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaningfulness**
- **person–job fit**
- **growth opportunities**

Below is the research-based picture.

---

## 1) Interesting work is strongly associated with higher job satisfaction

A foundational line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham, 1970s). It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

This model has held up reasonably well over decades.

### Stronger evidence: meta-analysis
A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed a very large work-design literature and found that enriching job characteristics—especially **autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback**—show **moderate to strong positive relationships** with:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- performance

and negative relationships with:

- burnout
- turnover intentions

In plain English:  
**people tend to be happier in jobs that are more engaging and better designed, not just better paid.**

---

## 2) Meaningful work predicts both work happiness and broader well-being

There is also a large “meaningful work” literature.

### Meta-analytic evidence
Meta-analyses such as **Allan et al.** found that **meaningful work** is associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- better **mental well-being**
- more **work engagement**
- less **distress**

The effect sizes are typically not tiny; they are often in the **moderate** range.

This matters because “interesting work” and “meaningful work” are not identical, but they overlap a lot in practice for knowledge workers. If a role is intellectually engaging and feels important, the literature predicts better day-to-day experience.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but actual pay level is a surprisingly weak predictor of job satisfaction

This is probably the clearest comparison to your question.

### Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
A well-known meta-analysis by **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the relationship between **how much people are paid** and **how satisfied they are with their jobs** is **positive but small**.

That is one of the most replicated findings in organizational psychology:
- **Pay matters**
- but **pay level alone explains surprisingly little** of job satisfaction compared with intrinsic features of the work

This does **not** mean money is unimportant. It means:
- a large pay shortfall hurts
- perceived unfairness hurts
- financial stress hurts
- but once compensation is already good, **marginal increases in salary often buy less satisfaction than better day-to-day work**

For someone already in a high-paying role, a “bit higher salary” is exactly the kind of case where the literature would predict a **modest** well-being gain from money, relative to a potentially larger effect from job quality.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to be better for sustained well-being than extrinsic rewards

**Self-Determination Theory** is relevant here. It distinguishes between:

- **autonomous/intrinsic motivation**: “I do this because it is interesting, important, or aligned with who I am”
- **controlled/extrinsic motivation**: “I do this for money, status, pressure, or external approval”

Across a large body of work, jobs that satisfy needs for:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

lead to better well-being, engagement, persistence, and lower burnout.

This does not mean extrinsic rewards are bad. It means that if a job is less interesting but pays more, the extra pay often does **not fully compensate psychologically** for lower autonomy/interest/meaning—unless the new role also satisfies some intrinsic need that matters a lot to you.

That last clause is important in your case.

---

## 5) Person–job fit matters a lot: “leading a team” may itself be intrinsically important

Research on **person–job fit** and **person–organization fit** finds that people do better when the role matches their motives, values, and preferred activities.

Meta-analytic work by **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** shows that better fit is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower turnover intention
- somewhat better performance

So there are really **two kinds of “interesting”** in your description:

1. **the subject matter/content** is more interesting at OpenAI  
2. **the role form** (leading a small team) may be more interesting at Anthropic

Research would not treat those as equivalent, but it would say both matter.  
If “I always wanted to lead a team” reflects a deep growth motive or identity-relevant goal, that can be a strong predictor of satisfaction in its own right.

So the literature does **not** say “always choose the technically more interesting work.”  
It says: **people do best when the job gives them the specific intrinsic rewards they personally value.**

---

## 6) What about regret? The evidence is weaker and more indirect

Here the research is less clean.

I’m not aware of a strong, famous longitudinal study that directly compares:
- “took the more interesting job”
vs
- “took the higher-paid but less interesting job”

and then measures regret years later.

But there are two relevant findings:

### A) Long-term regret often centers on missed personally meaningful opportunities
Research by **Gilovich & Medvec** and others suggests that over the long run, people often regret **inactions** and **missed opportunities**, especially those tied to their “ideal self.”

That implies—not proves—that people may regret **not pursuing work aligned with their deeper interests or aspirations** more than they regret not maximizing money.

### B) Regret is especially likely when choices violate core values/identity
Career regret studies generally point toward regret being strongest when people feel they chose against:
- their values
- their preferred identity
- their growth goals

So if someone cares a lot about interesting research and gives it up mainly for salary, that can become regret-inducing.  
But if someone deeply wants to build and lead, then **not taking a leadership opportunity** can also become a source of regret.

In other words, the regret literature supports **authenticity / value congruence**, more than either “follow the money” or “follow interest” in a generic sense.

---

## 7) A useful way to compare the factors, based on research

If you reduce the evidence to “how strongly does this usually predict well-being and job satisfaction?” the rough ranking is something like:

### Usually stronger predictors
- intrinsic interest in the work
- autonomy
- meaning / significance
- learning and growth
- person–job fit
- good colleagues/manager
- low chronic overload / burnout

### Usually weaker than people expect
- raw pay level, especially once already well compensated
- prestige alone
- external status

### Important but highly individual
- leadership opportunities
- advancement speed
- mission alignment
- company culture fit

That “highly individual” category matters for you because **managing a team** can be either:
- a major intrinsic motivator, or
- a trap that pulls time away from what you actually enjoy

Research would say the difference depends on fit.

---

## 8) If you want the most defensible research-based conclusion

A careful summary would be:

1. **Yes, people in more intrinsically interesting/meaningful jobs tend to be happier at work.**
2. These effects are generally **larger than the effects of pay level** on job satisfaction.
3. **Direct evidence on regret is thinner**, but long-term regret tends to be tied more to **value-incongruent choices** and **missed identity-relevant opportunities** than to forgone salary.
4. The biggest moderator is **fit**:
   - if the key intrinsic reward for you is doing fascinating research, staying in the more interesting role is what the literature would predict to be better for well-being.
   - if the key intrinsic reward is finally leading and building through others, the leadership role may be more satisfying despite less interesting technical content.

---

## 9) Papers / literatures worth looking up

If you want the strongest starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown et al. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit
- **Allan et al.** — meta-analysis on meaningful work
- **Self-Determination Theory** literature:
  - Deci & Ryan
  - work motivation / need satisfaction research
- **Gilovich & Medvec** — long-term regret / inaction regret

---

## 10) Bottom line in one sentence

**The research generally favors intrinsically interesting, meaningful, well-fitting work over modest increases in salary—but if leadership is itself a central intrinsic aspiration for you, that can be just as psychologically important as the interestingness of the technical work.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature**—for example, weighting interest, autonomy, meaning, leadership fit, salary, and regret risk according to what the evidence suggests.

answer:
Yes. The closest research answer is:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — how interesting, autonomous, challenging, meaningful, and well-matched the work is to your interests — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being** than **pay differences**, especially once pay is already high enough that money is not solving basic security problems.

The evidence is not usually framed as “interesting work vs. slightly higher salary at Anthropic/OpenAI,” but several well-developed literatures point in that direction.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly linked to job satisfaction

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually captured by things like:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance / meaning**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/master**

This comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** and later meta-analyses.

### Main result
Jobs high on those intrinsic characteristics tend to produce **higher job satisfaction, motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions**.

### Key sources
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** — classic work design theory.
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis supporting much of it.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — large meta-analysis showing that work design features like autonomy, variety, significance, and social support are meaningfully associated with satisfaction and motivation.

### Practical interpretation
If one job is “much more interesting” in the sense that it gives you more intellectual engagement, challenge, learning, and fit with your skills, the literature would generally predict **higher ongoing satisfaction** in that job.

---

## 2) Meaningful / intrinsically motivating work predicts well-being better than extrinsic rewards

A closely related literature is **Self-Determination Theory**.

It finds that well-being at work is higher when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work often increases the first two, and sometimes all three.

### Main result
When work satisfies those psychological needs, people report more:
- job satisfaction
- vitality
- engagement
- lower burnout

### Key sources
- **Deci & Ryan (2000)** — foundational self-determination theory.
- **Van den Broeck et al. (2016)** — review/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work.
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work and its outcomes.

### Practical interpretation
If “interesting” also means “this work feels alive / important / mentally engaging,” research generally says that matters a lot for happiness.

---

## 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect for job satisfaction

There is also a direct literature on **pay and job satisfaction**.

### Main result
The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is usually **positive but modest**.

That is: more money helps, but not by nearly as much as people often intuit.

### Key source
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

A rough summary of this literature is:
- pay has a real effect,
- but it is often **smaller than effects from work content, autonomy, supervisor quality, and fit**.

### Why?
Because salary is often:
- **less present in moment-to-moment experience** than the work itself,
- partly subject to **adaptation**,
- and often evaluated relatively (“am I being paid fairly?”) rather than absolutely.

---

## 4) At high income levels, extra money has diminishing returns

This is broader happiness research, not just job research.

### Main result
Additional income improves well-being, but with **diminishing marginal returns**. It tends to do more for:
- **life evaluation** (“my life is going well”)
than for
- **day-to-day emotional experience**.

There has been debate about exactly where the curve bends, but the broad conclusion is stable: **more money helps, but each extra increment helps less**, especially at already high incomes.

### Key sources
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — famous paper: income strongly affected life evaluation; emotional well-being showed flattening.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** — argued experienced well-being continues rising with income, though at diminishing rates.
- Later collaborative work between that camp and Kahneman’s camp narrowed the disagreement: the broad pattern is still “income helps, but the effect is not infinitely large and is smaller for people already doing well.”

### Practical interpretation
A **modest salary bump** is unlikely, on average, to compensate for **much worse daily work experience**, especially for someone already in a highly paid role.

---

## 5) Fit with your interests matters a lot

There is also a large literature on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

### Main result
When a job fits a person’s interests, values, and preferred way of working, that predicts:
- higher satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intention to leave
- better well-being

### Key source
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — major meta-analysis on fit.

There is also vocational-interest research showing that **interest congruence** is positively related to satisfaction and persistence.

### Practical interpretation
If “Anthropic is much less interesting” means a worse fit with what you naturally like doing, that is not a trivial preference. It is exactly the kind of variable that research says matters.

---

## 6) Leadership changes the equation — but only if leading is itself intrinsically important to you

This part is crucial.

You said that **leading a small team is something you always wanted to do**.

From a research perspective, that means leadership is **not just an extrinsic benefit**. It may satisfy intrinsic motives like:
- autonomy
- influence/impact
- growth
- identity
- mastery of a new domain

So the comparison is not simply:

> interesting work vs money

It may be:

> interesting technical work vs a role that is less interesting technically but more aligned with a long-standing aspiration for leadership

Research would say that **if leadership is genuinely part of your intrinsic motivation**, it can increase satisfaction despite the work being less inherently interesting.

But there is also a caution from research and practice literatures:
- first-line management often brings **more coordination**, **people problems**, **less maker time**, **more stress**, and **less direct immersion in the craft**.
- so whether it improves well-being depends strongly on whether you truly enjoy those activities, not just the title/status.

The empirical theme here is **fit**, not “management is better” or “IC is better.”

---

## 7) What about regret?

This is where the literature is thinner.

I’m not aware of a strong body of research that directly asks:

> Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over a somewhat higher-paying, more senior, less interesting one?

But related findings suggest:

### a) Career regret tends to be linked more to mismatch and lack of meaning than to modestly lower pay
Research on career satisfaction/regret usually finds stronger associations with:
- poor fit
- low meaning
- low growth
- low autonomy
than with small pay differences.

### b) Long-run regret research more generally often finds people regret not pursuing valued aspirations
In classic regret research, long-term regrets are often more about **inaction** or **not pursuing something important to one’s identity**, rather than narrowly economic tradeoffs.

### Key source
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** — classic long-term regret finding.

That doesn’t directly answer your exact question, but it suggests a useful research-based framing:

If **leadership** is a deeply valued aspiration, passing it up may create future regret.
If **intellectually interesting work** is core to your identity, leaving it may create future regret.

The research does **not** support a simple “higher salary = less regret later” story.

---

## 8) The strongest empirical summary

If I had to compress the literature into a few evidence-based points:

1. **Interesting, autonomous, meaningful work tends to predict job satisfaction more strongly than pay does.**
2. **A modest salary increase usually has a smaller effect on happiness than people expect, especially at already high compensation.**
3. **Person-job fit matters a lot.**
4. **Leadership can increase satisfaction if leadership itself is an intrinsic goal, not just a prestige marker.**
5. **There is limited direct evidence on regret, but long-term dissatisfaction/regret seems more tied to identity mismatch and unlived aspirations than to small salary deltas.**

---

## 9) If you want a research-grounded way to evaluate the choice

Not advice, just a way to map the literature onto your decision.

Rate each option on:

- **Interest / intellectual engagement**
- **Autonomy**
- **Learning / growth**
- **Meaning / impact**
- **Fit with your identity**
- **Fit with your long-term aspiration for leadership**
- **Daily work pattern**  
  - maker time vs meetings  
  - technical depth vs coordination  
  - people management burden
- **Social environment / manager / team quality**
- **Compensation / financial security**
- **Expected regret if you don’t take it**

The literature would generally predict that the variables near the top of that list matter more for ongoing satisfaction than the ones near the bottom — **unless** the leadership role is itself one of your strongest intrinsic goals.

---

## 10) If you want papers to look up first

A short reading list:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work.  
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987).** The validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A review and meta-analysis.  
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.  
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000).** The “what” and “why” of goal pursuits.  
- **Van den Broeck, A., et al. (2016).** A review of self-determination theory’s basic psychological needs at work.  
- **Judge, T. A., et al. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.  
- **Allan, B. A., et al. (2019).** Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.  
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.  
- **Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010).** High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.  
- **Killingsworth, M. A. (2021).** Experienced well-being rises with income.  
- **Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1995).** The experience of regret.

If you want, I can also do one of two more focused things:

1. give you a **very concise literature summary with strongest findings only**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision matrix based strictly on empirical predictors of job satisfaction/regret**.
