answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**People are usually happier in jobs that are intrinsically interesting/engaging, meaningful, and autonomous, and those factors often predict job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than pay alone.**  
The “regret” literature is less direct, but the evidence we do have suggests people often **adapt faster to salary/title gains** than to the **daily lived experience of the work itself**.

A careful summary:

---

## 1) What research uses as the closest proxy for “interesting work”

Academically, “interesting work” is usually broken into constructs like:

- **Intrinsic motivation** — the work is engaging in itself
- **Job characteristics** — variety, challenge, significance, feedback, autonomy
- **Meaningful work** — feeling the work matters
- **Person-job fit** — the work matches your interests/strengths
- **Need satisfaction at work** — especially autonomy and competence

These are not identical, but they all point in the same direction.

---

## 2) Strong evidence: intrinsic job quality matters a lot for happiness at work

### A. Job characteristics / work design
The classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. It argues that jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

This is not just theory. A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  found that these kinds of job features are **strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, commitment, and lower turnover intentions**.

The basic result from this whole literature is very robust:  
**what the work feels like day-to-day matters a great deal.**

### B. Self-determination theory
Another large literature, from **self-determination theory** (Deci, Ryan; work applications by Gagné and others), finds that well-being at work is higher when people experience:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Of those, for your question, **autonomy and competence** are especially relevant.  
Work that is interesting, challenging, and lets you exercise your abilities tends to produce better well-being than work that is merely higher-paid but less intrinsically rewarding.

There are meta-analytic reviews showing that need satisfaction at work predicts:

- greater engagement
- greater job satisfaction
- lower burnout
- better general well-being

---

## 3) Meaningful work also has a strong relationship with satisfaction and well-being

There is now a fairly large literature on **meaningful work**. Meta-analyses and systematic reviews generally find that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life satisfaction
- organizational commitment
- lower intention to quit

This isn’t exactly the same as “interesting,” but in knowledge work the two often overlap: the work is absorbing because it feels valuable, intellectually alive, or identity-relevant.

For research-oriented people, this can matter even more because identity and work are often tightly linked.

---

## 4) Pay matters — but usually less than people think for satisfaction once you’re already well-paid

A very relevant result:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction**

Their conclusion was basically:

- the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but relatively modest**
- higher pay helps, but it does **not dominate** job satisfaction

In plainer language:

- If money changes **financial security**, that matters a lot.
- But once you are already in a comfortable/high-income range, **extra salary usually has a smaller effect on day-to-day job happiness** than people expect.

This fits a much wider literature in economics and psychology:
people care a lot about money, but **non-monetary job attributes** often have surprisingly large effects on well-being.

---

## 5) Autonomy/control is one of the most important predictors of well-being

This is especially relevant because your options differ not only in salary and interest, but also in **role structure**.

A large body of work on **job control** (Karasek; Whitehall studies; occupational health literature) shows that **low control** is associated with:

- worse mental well-being
- higher stress
- more burnout risk
- worse physical health outcomes in some studies

High-control roles are generally better tolerated, even when demanding.

So if “leading a small team” means **more autonomy, influence, and control**, that is a genuine positive in the research literature.  
But if it means **more meetings, less actual research, more people-management stress**, then it may reduce the very things that make the work satisfying.

That is: **leadership can improve satisfaction, but only if it aligns with your interests and values**.

---

## 6) Person-job fit matters a lot

A strong meta-analysis here is:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** on person-environment fit

The broad result: when people’s jobs fit their interests, values, and abilities, they report:

- higher job satisfaction
- better commitment
- lower turnover intentions

This matters because the answer is not just “interesting work beats pay” in general. It is more like:

> **The more central intellectual interest and mission fit are to your identity, the more costly it is to give them up.**

For some people, management responsibility is energizing.  
For others, losing direct contact with the craft is costly.  
The literature would predict that this **fit issue** matters a lot.

---

## 7) What about regret specifically?

This part is less clean. There is **less direct research on “career regret after choosing the less interesting but better-paying role”** than there is on satisfaction and well-being.

But several adjacent findings are relevant:

### A. People often mispredict what will matter
Research on **affective forecasting** shows people often overestimate the lasting happiness impact of salient external changes like:

- salary
- title
- prestige

and underestimate the impact of **repeated daily experience**.

In career terms:
people may overweight “I became a team lead at a more prestigious frontier lab with more pay” and underweight “I spend every day doing work I find less compelling.”

### B. Adaptation is often faster for pay/status than for task content
The general well-being literature suggests that people often adapt to external gains relatively quickly.  
By contrast, the **ongoing texture of daily work** — whether it is absorbing, frustrating, meaningful, bureaucratic, creative — keeps affecting experience.

So the research-based expectation would be:

- salary bump: real benefit, but often partly adapted to
- role prestige: also adapted to
- chronic work interestingness / autonomy / fit: often remains important because it is repeatedly experienced

### C. Job-change satisfaction tends to depend a lot on whether the new role improves intrinsic conditions
Longitudinal job-change studies generally find that satisfaction after switching depends heavily on whether the new role improves things like:

- fit
- autonomy
- management quality
- meaning
- stress level

not just compensation.

So the indirect answer on regret is:
**people are more likely to regret changes that improved extrinsic rewards but worsened the lived quality of the work.**

---

## 8) If I had to summarize the research in one hierarchy

For long-run job satisfaction and well-being, the literature usually points to something like this:

### Very important
- **Interesting/engaging work**
- **Autonomy/control**
- **Good fit with your strengths/interests**
- **Meaningfulness**
- **Manager/colleague quality**

### Important, but usually smaller than people intuit
- **Pay level**, especially once already financially comfortable
- **Title/status**

### Can go either way depending on fit
- **Leadership opportunity**
  - good if you genuinely want to build/manage
  - bad if it removes the parts of work you most enjoy

---

## 9) So: do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes — if “more interesting” also means more intrinsically motivating, better fit, and not dramatically worse on other dimensions.**

The evidence does **not** say “always choose the most interesting work regardless of everything else.”  
But it does say that:

1. **Interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being**
2. **Pay has a positive but smaller effect than many people expect**
3. **Autonomy and fit are especially important**
4. **People likely underweight daily work experience when making career choices**

---

## 10) The most relevant papers/literatures to look at

If you want starting points:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976/1980)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design/job characteristics

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

- **Whitehall / job control literature** — autonomy/control and stress/health
- Recent **meaningful work meta-analyses** (Allan and colleagues; related reviews)

---

1. **Give you a tighter “research digest” with 8–12 specific studies and their findings**, or  
2. **Translate the literature into a decision framework** specifically for your case: interesting research vs higher pay vs first team-lead opportunity.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, and the broad pattern is fairly consistent:

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and labor economics, **intrinsic qualities of work** — things like **interest, meaning, autonomy, challenge, learning, and fit with your values/identity** — tend to be **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and engagement** than **pay level alone**.

So if you ask, “Do people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier?” the research answer is roughly **yes, usually**.

If you ask, “Do they regret that choice later?” the evidence is **less direct**, but long-term regret research suggests people often regret **missing identity-relevant opportunities** more than missing incremental money.

The important complication in your case is that **leading a team** may itself be an intrinsic aspiration, not just an extrinsic perk. So the choice may not be “interesting work vs money,” but rather **research-interest vs leadership-interest**.

---

## What the research says

### 1. Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A very old but still influential line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later supported by large meta-analyses.

The core idea: jobs are more satisfying when they provide things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- a sense of ownership/identity

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
  Found that motivational work characteristics such as **autonomy, task variety, significance, and feedback** are strongly associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **internal motivation**
  - higher **engagement**
  - lower **withdrawal/stress**

This is probably the closest research analogue to “interesting work.”

### 2. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

Another relevant literature is on **meaningful work**.

- **Allan et al. (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
  Found that meaningful work is associated with:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - higher **work engagement**
  - higher **life satisfaction**
  - lower **turnover intentions**

The effects here are usually **moderate to fairly large** by social-science standards.

So if one job feels “much less interesting,” and that also means “less meaningful” or “less aligned with what I find intellectually alive,” the literature would generally predict **lower day-to-day work well-being**.

### 3. Fit matters a lot

A very robust finding is that **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** matter.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, meta-analysis  
  Better fit predicts:
  - higher **job satisfaction**
  - stronger **commitment**
  - lower **intentions to quit**

This is especially relevant if your current role fits your identity as a researcher, while the alternative fits a different self-concept (manager/leader).

A key implication:  
People are not just happier in “good jobs” in the abstract — they’re happier in jobs that fit **what they actually value**.

### 4. Salary matters, but its effect on job satisfaction is smaller than most people expect

There is direct research on this:

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
  The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.

The exact number varies by method, but the basic conclusion is stable:  
**Higher pay helps, but not nearly as much as people often assume.**

So if both jobs already pay well, then an incremental increase in salary usually has **less effect on happiness** than differences in:
- intrinsic interest
- autonomy
- meaning
- fit
- manager/teammate quality

### 5. Income improves life evaluation, but daily emotional well-being shows diminishing returns

Relevant economics/well-being work:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: higher income strongly improves **life evaluation**, but daily emotional well-being showed a plateau in their data.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** challenged the hard plateau and found well-being continuing to rise with income.
- Later reconciliation work suggests the truth is more nuanced: **income generally helps**, but gains are often **diminishing**, especially once basic needs and comfort are well covered.

For someone choosing between two already high-paying technical jobs, the literature would generally suggest:

- extra pay may improve your sense that you’re doing well,
- but **the lived texture of the workday** is more likely to determine whether you enjoy your life.

### 6. Intrinsic goals tend to be better for well-being than extrinsic goals

This is from self-determination theory and adjacent work:

- **Kasser & Ryan** and related research on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations**
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** / broader self-determination theory work

General finding:
People oriented more toward **intrinsic motives** — growth, mastery, connection, meaning — tend to report better well-being than people focused mainly on **extrinsic motives** like money, status, image.

That does **not** mean money/status don’t matter. It means that when they become the dominant basis for a decision, they are usually weaker predictors of later satisfaction than intrinsic fit.

---

## What about becoming a manager / leading a small team?

Here the evidence is more mixed.

There isn’t a simple “managers are happier” result. Leadership roles can bring:

### Potential upsides
- more **autonomy**
- more **influence**
- more **status**
- more sense of growth if leadership is an important aspiration

### Potential downsides
- more **role overload**
- more **administrative burden**
- more **emotional labor**
- less time doing the craft itself
- worse fit if you like creating/researching more than coordinating

The research is pretty clear that **fit to the role** matters a lot.  
If leading people is a deep, identity-level aspiration for you, then that is not “just a perk” — it is part of the intrinsic value of the job.

In other words:

- If you mainly love **doing research**, a less interesting manager role may lower satisfaction.
- If you deeply want to **build and lead**, then the Anthropic offer may actually be more intrinsically rewarding despite less interesting technical content.

This is why the relevant research variable is often not “salary vs interest,” but **which job better satisfies your core motives**.

---

## What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence, because there’s less direct work on “regret after choosing a more interesting vs more lucrative job.”

But there are two relevant findings:

### 1. Long-term regret often centers on missed opportunities, not just bad actions
- **Gilovich & Medvec (1994, 1995)**  
  People tend to regret **inactions** more than actions over the long term.

### 2. Regrets cluster around domains tied to the “ideal self”
- **Roese & Summerville (2005)**  
  Regret tends to be strongest in domains where people perceive large unrealized opportunities.

Applied carefully, that suggests:
- If “leading a team” is a long-standing part of your ideal future self, passing it up may be something you could regret.
- If “doing genuinely interesting research” is central to your identity, giving that up may also create regret.

So regret research does **not** say “take the higher-status option.”  
It says regret often comes from failing to pursue what feels central to who you want to become.

---

## If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

**Between two already well-paid jobs, the one with greater intrinsic interest, meaning, autonomy, and personal fit will usually produce higher sustained job satisfaction than the one with somewhat higher salary — unless the alternative fulfills another deeply important intrinsic motive, such as a genuine desire to lead.**

---

## The most relevant papers/research streams

If you want to look them up:

1. **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model  
2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit  
4. **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction  
5. **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work  
6. **Gagné & Deci (2005)** / broader **Self-Determination Theory** at work  
7. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**; **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and well-being  
8. **Gilovich & Medvec (1994/1995)** — action vs inaction regret  
9. **Roese & Summerville (2005)** — domains of regret

---

## Bottom line from the research

- **Yes**, more interesting/meaningful work is usually associated with greater happiness at work.
- **Yes**, those effects are often stronger than pay differences.
- **No**, there isn’t strong direct evidence that “choosing interesting work” systematically leads to regret.
- **But** regret is more likely when you ignore something central to your identity — and in your case that could be either **research depth** or **leadership**.

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision memo with actual paper citations and a research-weighted framework** specifically for your choice:  
**interesting individual-contributor work vs less interesting work + first team leadership opportunity**.

answer:
Yes. The broad answer from organizational psychology is:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, challenging, and growth-promoting the work is — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and engagement than pay level alone**, especially once pay is already high enough that basic financial concerns are not the main issue.

The important caveat is that **“leading a team” is not just an extrinsic reward**. In the research literature, that can itself be an intrinsic good if it satisfies values like autonomy, impact, growth, mastery, or generativity. So your tradeoff is probably not just:

- interesting work vs more money

but more like:

- **interesting technical/research work** vs **leadership/impact/advancement**, plus some money.

## What the research says

### 1) Job design / “interesting work” matters a lot

The classic line of research here is the **Job Characteristics Model** and later work-design meta-analyses.

Key job features that consistently predict higher motivation and satisfaction:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance**
- **Task identity**
- **Feedback**
- Sometimes also **social support** and manageable demands

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** found that work-design characteristics were strongly related to **job satisfaction, internal motivation, work engagement, commitment, and lower burnout/withdrawal**.

Older meta-analytic support:

- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** also found meaningful links between enriched job characteristics and outcomes like satisfaction and motivation.

So if by “interesting” you mean some combination of:
- intellectually engaging,
- high learning,
- creative,
- varied,
- consequential,
- autonomous,

then the literature is quite favorable to staying in that kind of role.

### 2) Pay matters, but its link to job satisfaction is modest

A useful comparison:

- **Judge et al. (2010)**: the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small**.

That’s one of the more robust findings in this area: higher pay helps, but it usually does **less** for satisfaction than people expect.

This does **not** mean money is irrelevant. It matters for:
- financial security,
- optionality,
- family constraints,
- status,
- reducing stress,
- life evaluation.

But in terms of **day-to-day enjoyment and satisfaction with work**, the content of the job often matters more than the pay differential once both options are already well-paid.

### 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**.

Recent meta-analytic work finds that meaningful work is associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **life satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower **burnout/depressive symptoms** (association, not always causal)

A review/meta-analysis often cited here is:

- **Allan et al.** on meaningful work and related outcomes

If the OpenAI role feels much more meaningful/interesting to you, that is not a trivial preference — it’s exactly the kind of variable that predicts better subjective outcomes in the literature.

### 4) Intrinsic motivation matters especially for creative/research work

For complex, creative, knowledge-intensive work, the evidence tends to favor intrinsic motivation even more strongly.

Relevant line of work:

- **Amabile**’s research on creativity
- **Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)** meta-analysis on intrinsic motivation and performance

The broad result is that **intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality and persistence in complex tasks**, whereas extrinsic incentives are less dominant than people assume.

That’s especially relevant for research engineering, where the quality of thinking and sustained curiosity matter.

### 5) Person-job fit and value congruence are very strong predictors

This is one of the most important literatures for your case.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis: **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit** are strongly associated with **job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower intent to quit**.

This matters because there is no universal answer like “interesting work always beats leadership” or “leadership always beats interesting work.”

What predicts better outcomes is often:
- whether the role fits your values,
- your identity,
- your strengths,
- what you want your days to feel like.

So if “leading a small team” is something you genuinely care about, that may raise the fit of the Anthropic role even if the subject matter is less intrinsically interesting.

### 6) Promotions/leadership do not reliably increase happiness unless they fit the person

The research here is less clean than for pay or job design, but the general pattern is:

- Promotions often increase **pay, status, and responsibility**
- Their effect on **subjective well-being** is often **mixed, modest, or temporary**
- Much depends on whether the new role matches what the person actually wants

People sometimes assume “more senior” or “managerial” means “better,” but the evidence is much more conditional than that.

If leadership is genuinely a desired form of work, it can increase satisfaction.
If it mainly means less of what you enjoy and more coordination/admin/conflict management, it can reduce satisfaction despite higher compensation/status.

## Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job?

Here the evidence is weaker.

I don’t know of a strong, clean body of longitudinal research showing:
- “people who chose interesting jobs later regretted it less than people who chose higher-paying jobs.”

The regret literature is more general than career-specific.

What we *do* know indirectly is:

- people do worse when they choose roles misaligned with their core values or identity;
- value-congruent, self-concordant goals tend to predict better well-being;
- people often overestimate the lasting happiness gains from external rewards like pay/status.

So the research-backed statement is **not**:
> “People never regret choosing interesting work.”

It is more like:
> “On average, intrinsic qualities of work and value-fit predict sustained job satisfaction better than pay alone, and there is limited evidence that higher pay/status compensate for poor fit or low interest.”

## Probably the most relevant synthesis for your exact situation

If I translate the literature into your choice, the key questions are:

1. **How much less interesting is Anthropic, really?**
   - Small difference or huge difference?
   - Research suggests a large drop in intrinsic interest is costly.

2. **Is leading a team an intrinsic aspiration for you or mainly a prestige signal?**
   - If it’s something you’ve “always wanted,” the literature would treat that as meaningful, not just extrinsic.

3. **What would your day-to-day work actually be?**
   - Research predicts happiness from daily task characteristics more than from the abstract title.

4. **How large is the salary difference?**
   - If both roles are already very highly paid, the marginal happiness gain from more money is likely smaller than the gain/loss from work content and fit.

5. **Which role better satisfies autonomy, competence, meaning, and fit?**
   - That bundle predicts a lot.

## Bottom line

The research does **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.”
But it does say, fairly consistently:

- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work strongly predicts job satisfaction and engagement**
- **Pay has a positive but relatively modest relationship with job satisfaction**
- **At high income levels, marginal pay increases usually matter less than people expect**
- **Leadership can increase satisfaction if it is genuinely aligned with your values**
- **Fit matters a lot; direct regret evidence is sparse, but value-incongruent choices are riskier**

So if you want the most research-grounded summary in one line:

> **People are generally happier in jobs that are intrinsically engaging and well-matched to their values, and that effect is often stronger than the effect of somewhat higher pay. But if leadership itself is one of your core intrinsic values, then the leadership opportunity may be more important than “interesting subject matter” alone.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature** — e.g. score each option on autonomy, meaning, interest, growth, leadership fit, compensation, and likely long-term well-being.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not much that studies your exact choice head-on. The closest literatures are on:

- **intrinsic job characteristics**: interesting/challenging work, autonomy, learning, meaning
- **extrinsic rewards**: salary, status, promotion
- **person-job/person-organization fit**
- **regret over career decisions**
- **management/leadership roles**

### Short version

The best-supported finding is:

> **Once compensation is already “good enough,” the intrinsic quality of the work — especially interest, autonomy, learning, meaning, and fit — predicts job satisfaction and well-being more strongly than pay level alone.**

But your case has an important complication:

> **“Leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk; it can itself be an intrinsic aspiration.**  
If leadership is something you’ve genuinely wanted, then the management role may satisfy an important “ideal self” motive, which matters both for satisfaction and for long-term regret.

So the literature does **not** say “always choose the more interesting individual-contributor role.” It says that people tend to do best when the job fits their deeper motives, and **interest/meaning/fit usually matter more than extra salary**, but **missed identity-relevant aspirations** (like “I always wanted to lead”) are also a common source of regret.

---

## What the research says

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly tied to satisfaction and motivation

In organizational psychology, “interesting” is usually broken into things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **challenge / complexity**
- **opportunities to learn**

This goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** and has been supported many times since.

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** reviewed work design research and found that job characteristics like **autonomy, task variety, and task significance** have **substantial positive associations** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - internal motivation
  - engagement
  - lower strain / better psychological outcomes

These effects are typically **moderate by social-science standards**, and often larger than the effects of pay level.

### Interpretation
If one role is clearly more intellectually engaging, more autonomously motivating, and more meaningful to you, the literature would predict **higher day-to-day work satisfaction** in that role.

---

## 2) Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of positive work outcomes

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al.** on the outcomes of meaningful work found that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **overall well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**

This is one of the most consistent findings in work psychology: people do better when their work feels worth doing, not just well-paid.

### Interpretation
If by “interesting” you partly mean “this work feels genuinely important/exciting,” that matters a lot in the literature.

---

## 3) Pay matters — but less than most people expect for job satisfaction

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al. (2010)** found that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest** — around **r ≈ .15**.

That’s not zero. Money matters. But it is **far from dominant**.

Important nuances:

- Pay affects **security**, **optionality**, and often **status**
- It tends to matter more when it relieves real constraints
- Once income is already high, the incremental psychological benefit of “a bit more” is often smaller than people predict
- **Relative pay** and fairness often matter as much as absolute pay

There is also the broader well-being literature:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**: income strongly relates to **life evaluation**, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns
- **Killingsworth (2021)**: found continued gains with higher income, though with diminishing marginal value and plenty of variation

### Interpretation
Higher salary does improve things, but the research does **not** support the idea that modest pay increases usually compensate for a meaningful drop in work interest or fit.

---

## 4) Fit is often more predictive than any single factor

A strong meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** found that:

- **person-job fit**
- **person-organization fit**

predict:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- intention to stay
- performance

This is very relevant here. A role can be “better on paper” (more money, more status, leadership title) and still be worse for you if it’s a poorer fit with your interests, values, and preferred way of working.

### Interpretation
The research usually favors **fit over prestige**.

---

## 5) Intrinsic aspirations tend to be better for well-being than extrinsic ones

From **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) and related goal-content research:

- **intrinsic motives** — growth, mastery, contribution, affiliation, autonomy — are more reliably linked to well-being
- **extrinsic motives** — money, status, image — can motivate behavior, but are generally weaker predictors of sustained well-being

In work settings, people tend to be better off when their job satisfies:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

These are among the most replicated findings in motivational psychology.

### Interpretation
If your attraction to Anthropic is mostly “slightly higher pay + title,” the literature would treat that as weaker.  
If your attraction is “I deeply want to become a leader and mentor people,” that is **not** just extrinsic — it may be a core intrinsic aspiration.

---

## 6) Leadership roles are mixed for well-being; aspiration-fit matters a lot

The literature on management/leadership is less clean than the literatures above.

What tends to be true:

- management roles often bring **more autonomy, influence, status**
- but also **more role conflict, ambiguity, emotional labor, and stress**
- first-line management is often harder than people imagine

So, on average, “becoming a manager” is **not automatically a happiness upgrade**.

However, the best predictor is usually **fit**:
- if someone **wants** to lead, enjoys coaching/coordination, and sees leadership as meaningful growth, the role can be very rewarding
- if someone mainly loves technical/deep work and is pushed into management, satisfaction often drops

### Interpretation
The research would say: the key question is not “is leadership objectively better?” but “is leadership part of your actual motivational profile?”

In your case, since you said it’s “something I always wanted to do,” that is psychologically important.

---

## 7) On regret: people often regret not pursuing identity-relevant opportunities

The regret literature is relevant, though it’s less job-specific.

Classic findings from **Gilovich & Medvec** and later work:

- In the **short run**, people often regret actions
- In the **long run**, people more often regret **inactions**
- The most enduring regrets tend to involve the **ideal self**:
  - the person one hoped to become
  - missed growth opportunities
  - education/career/relationship paths not tried

Career and education are among the most common domains of major life regrets.

### Important nuance
This does **not** mean “always take the risky opportunity.”  
But it does mean that if leadership is part of your “ideal self,” then **never trying it** is exactly the sort of thing that long-term regret research says can linger.

By contrast, people less often have enduring regret over passing up a **modest salary increment**, unless the money would have changed something major.

---

# So: do people who stay in the more interesting job tend to be happier?

### On average: **yes, if “interesting” means**
- more intrinsically motivating
- more meaningful
- more aligned with values
- more autonomy / learning / challenge

Those factors are robustly associated with better job satisfaction and often broader well-being.

### But the major caveat is:
If the “less interesting” job gives you something that is itself a deep intrinsic goal — e.g. **leadership, mentorship, building a team** — then it’s no longer a simple “interesting vs salary” tradeoff.

It becomes more like:

- **interesting research / intellectual engagement**
vs
- **leadership aspiration / identity growth**

That is a much closer call in the literature.

---

# If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence

A research-based summary would be:

> **For long-run job happiness, intrinsic quality of work and fit usually matter more than incremental pay; but people also regret not pursuing core aspirations, and “I always wanted to lead” is exactly the kind of aspiration the regret literature says can matter later.**

---

# Practical research-grounded questions to ask yourself

Not personal advice — just the variables the literature says matter most:

1. **Which role gives you better day-to-day experience?**
   - autonomy
   - interest
   - challenge
   - flow
   - learning

2. **Which role better fits your identity?**
   - “What kind of person am I trying to become?”
   - deep technical researcher?
   - research leader / builder of teams?

3. **How large is the actual compensation delta in lived terms?**
   - materially life-changing?
   - or just locally higher?

4. **How reversible is the choice?**
   - regret is lower when choices are easier to revisit
   - could you try management and return to IC work later?

5. **Is the leadership role truly meaningful to you, or mainly attractive because it is a prestigious next step?**
   - the literature treats these differently

---

# If you want the most defensible bottom-line reading

If both jobs are already elite/high-comp roles, the evidence generally favors:

- **interest**
- **meaning**
- **autonomy**
- **fit**

over

- **modest increases in pay**

But in your specific description, the strongest research-based reason to seriously consider Anthropic is **not the extra salary**. It is that **leading a team appears to be a longstanding aspiration**, and missed identity-relevant aspirations are exactly the sort of thing people later dwell on.

---

If you want, I can give you:
1. a **short annotated bibliography** of the key papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **decision matrix based only on research-backed factors**.

**Exploring goals and well-being**

It seems that extrinsic goals like money and fame are associated with lower well-being, while intrinsic goals such as personal growth and close relationships contribute positively. There's not much direct research on job choice-specific regret, but studies like Kasser & Ryan (1993/1996) and Dittmar et al. (2014) indicate that prioritizing extrinsic rewards often leads to lower well-being. I wonder if the user values aspects like team leadership, which might satisfy deeper needs for competence and relatedness. This suggests that understanding fit and value congruence is important when assessing job satisfaction.
**Evaluating job satisfaction metrics**

I’m thinking about wrapping this up by saying I can turn the information into a weighted scorecard if it’s helpful. I probably shouldn’t mention specific affiliations too much. It’s important to avoid any “hallucinated” exact effect sizes unless I’m fairly sure about them. For example, previous studies show a corrected correlation of around .15 for pay level and job satisfaction. Meanwhile, meaningful work correlates around .49. Autonomy is usually a strong predictor in the .4 range, with other factors like task significance and social support also playing a role.
**Preparing structured response**

I’m considering citing “Van den Broeck et al., 2016” as a review, but it's not a meta-analysis. The “Slemp et al. 2018” meta-analysis on job crafting could be useful. I think I can incorporate evidence from self-determination theory without needing to rely on exact numbers. To make it clear and organized, I’ll prepare my response with distinct sections: a short answer first, and then I need to decide what to include next.
**Exploring work motivations**

I'm considering the findings about workers in organizations with a social mission, like the "Burbano 2016" study, suggesting they accept lower wages. There's field evidence supporting this, like the idea of "mission as a worker motivator." I could cite this study, but it might feel like overkill.  

Older studies, like the International Social Survey Programme, show that interesting work and job security rank high among workers' values.  

I want to keep it concise and research-focused, highlighting causality caution while crafting a clear summary.
answer:
Yes. The short version from the literature is:

- **“Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.**
- **Pay matters, but its direct relationship with job satisfaction is usually much smaller than people expect**, especially once pay is already high enough that it doesn’t create financial strain or a sense of unfairness.
- **There is less direct research on “regret” specifically**, but the evidence on longitudinal well-being, burnout, turnover, and value mismatch points in the same direction: people tend to do worse when they choose jobs that are less intrinsically rewarding *for them*, even if extrinsic rewards are better.
- A big qualifier: **person–job fit matters a lot.** If “leading a team” is itself a major intrinsic goal for you, that is not just an extrinsic perk; it can be a meaningful source of autonomy, growth, and purpose.

## What the research most consistently finds

### 1) Intrinsic job qualities matter a lot
In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is usually studied under things like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** such as skill variety, autonomy, task significance, feedback
- **need satisfaction** at work (autonomy, competence, relatedness)

These are **reliably and strongly associated** with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower burnout
- lower turnover intentions
- better overall well-being

A foundational framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)** proposed that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:
  - skill variety
  - task identity
  - task significance
  - autonomy
  - feedback

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis of work design:
  - Found that job characteristics like **autonomy, task significance, skill variety, and feedback** had **moderate positive associations** with job satisfaction and motivation.
  - In practical terms: work that feels substantive, varied, and self-directed is consistently linked to being happier at work.

Another major line:

- **Meaningful work** has been shown to be strongly related to job satisfaction and well-being.
- **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work found that meaningful work is **moderately to strongly associated** with:
  - job satisfaction
  - work engagement
  - life satisfaction
  - lower distress/burnout

If I had to summarize the literature in one sentence: **interesting/meaningful work is not a “nice extra”; it is a central driver of how people experience their jobs.**

---

### 2) Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic work quality
The best-known result here is:

- **Judge et al. (2010)**, meta-analysis on **pay level and job satisfaction**:
  - The relationship is **positive but fairly small**.
  - The corrected correlation is usually reported around **~.15**.

That is not zero. But compared with many intrinsic job characteristics, it is **materially smaller**.

A few related points from the literature:

- **Perceived fairness/equity of pay** often matters more than absolute pay.
- Pay has more impact when:
  - it changes financial security,
  - it changes status in a personally meaningful way,
  - or it resolves a sense of being underpaid.
- For already highly compensated workers, **a modest salary increase often has limited effect on day-to-day work happiness** relative to changes in the actual content of the work.

This is also consistent with broader happiness research: extra income tends to help less at higher income levels than at lower ones. The exact shape is debated, but **diminishing marginal returns** is a robust broad idea.

---

### 3) Value congruence / fit is crucial
One of the strongest findings across career and organizational research is that **fit** matters:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** meta-analysis on person–environment fit:
  - Better fit is associated with higher job satisfaction, stronger commitment, and lower intention to quit.

This matters because your choice is not simply:

- interesting work vs money

It is more like:

- interesting work,
- leadership opportunity,
- status,
- mission alignment,
- growth,
- compensation,
- and your own values.

If **leading a team** is something you “always wanted,” that is important in the research sense, not just personally. It may reflect:
- a growth motive,
- desire for impact,
- autonomy,
- competence,
- broader scope,
- or meaning through mentoring.

So from a research perspective, that part belongs on the **intrinsic side** too, not only the extrinsic side.

---

### 4) Research on “regret” is thinner than research on satisfaction
There is much less clean evidence specifically asking:

> “Do people regret staying in the more interesting job?”

What the literature mostly gives instead is indirect evidence through:

- later job satisfaction
- later well-being
- burnout
- turnover
- value conflict
- career fit

The pattern is roughly:

- Choosing work that is **less intrinsically motivating** tends to predict lower satisfaction and higher desire to leave.
- Choosing paths driven mainly by **extrinsic rewards** over intrinsic values is often associated with lower well-being.

Related evidence from **self-determination theory** and the intrinsic-vs-extrinsic aspirations literature:
- People who orient more toward **intrinsic goals** (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) generally show better well-being than those oriented more toward **extrinsic goals** (money, image, status), all else equal.
- This doesn’t mean money or status are bad.
- It means that **when choices are made primarily at the expense of intrinsic motives**, outcomes tend to be worse.

So while “regret” evidence is not especially direct, the best available evidence points to this:  
**people are more likely to thrive in jobs that fit their intrinsic motives and values, and less likely to thrive in jobs chosen mainly for external rewards.**

---

## A rough hierarchy from the research
Very roughly, for predicting happiness at work:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Fit with your values and strengths**
4. **Relationships / manager / culture**
5. **Fair and sufficient pay**
6. Additional pay beyond that

That is an oversimplification, but it is a pretty fair summary of the literature.

---

## Important nuance for your exact situation
Your case has a subtlety:

- The **OpenAI role** sounds stronger on **interest/content**
- The **Anthropic role** sounds stronger on **leadership opportunity** and somewhat on pay

From a research perspective, leadership opportunity is not just “more money.” It could increase:

- autonomy
- impact
- status
- meaning
- growth
- identity coherence (“this is the kind of role I’ve wanted”)

But it could also reduce satisfaction if it means:
- less hands-on technical work,
- more coordination/admin,
- less research depth,
- or less intrinsic interest in the day-to-day tasks.

So the most research-consistent question is not:

> Is more interesting work always better than more pay?

It is:

> Which option gives me the better bundle of intrinsic rewards that I personally value most?

Because **interesting work and desired leadership are both intrinsic-ish variables**, and both usually matter more than a modest pay delta.

---

## If you want the strongest single empirical takeaway
If you forced me to compress the literature into one sentence:

> **On average, people are happier in jobs that are more intrinsically motivating/meaningful/autonomous, and those factors usually predict job satisfaction more strongly than modest differences in pay.**

That is the clearest research-backed answer.

---

## Representative references
A non-exhaustive list you could look up:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).** Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory.  
- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1980).** *Work Redesign.*
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary. *Journal of Applied Psychology.*
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis. *Journal of Vocational Behavior.*
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis. *Personnel Psychology.*
- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010).** On the meaning of work: A theoretical integration and review. *Research in Organizational Behavior.*
- **Allan, B. A.** and colleagues on meaningful work and well-being/meta-analytic reviews.
- For broader motivation theory: **Deci & Ryan** on **Self-Determination Theory**.

---

If you want, I can do one of two more research-focused follow-ups:

1. **Give you a short annotated bibliography** with the most relevant papers and one-line summaries, or  
2. **Translate the literature into a decision framework** for your specific choice, while still keeping it evidence-based rather than opinion-based.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**On average, more interesting/intrinsically rewarding work is a stronger predictor of sustained job satisfaction than modest differences in pay.**  
But the key moderating variable is **fit**: if the new role satisfies a different intrinsic motive you care a lot about—like leading/mentoring/building an org—then it can absolutely beat a more technically interesting IC role.

A good way to summarize the literature is:

1. **Intrinsic job characteristics matter a lot**
2. **Pay matters, but less than people often expect**
3. **Meaning and fit matter a lot**
4. **Promotion/leadership is not automatically a well-being upgrade; it depends heavily on fit**
5. **Direct evidence on “regret” is thinner than evidence on satisfaction/well-being**

## 1) Interesting work: strong predictor of job satisfaction

A large organizational-psychology literature treats “interesting work” as things like:

- skill variety
- challenge/complexity
- autonomy
- feedback
- task significance
- learning/growth

This goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and multiple meta-analyses support it.

### What the evidence says
Meta-analytic work generally finds that these job-design features have **moderate to strong associations** with:

- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

Among these, **autonomy**, **variety/complexity**, and **meaningfulness** are especially robust.

A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: integrated work-design research and found that motivational job characteristics are strongly related to job satisfaction, motivation, and performance-related outcomes.

An earlier one:
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)**: meta-analysis supporting the Job Characteristics Model, especially for satisfaction and motivation.

So if by “interesting” you mean “I actually enjoy the substance of what I do, it stretches me, I learn, and it feels meaningful,” then yes: **that predicts happiness at work quite well**.

## 2) Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic job quality

There is a clear finding here: **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction, but the relationship is positive and fairly small** compared with many intrinsic job factors.

A commonly cited meta-analysis:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** found the relationship between pay level and job satisfaction to be **small**.

The practical implication is not “money doesn’t matter.” It does. But:
- once compensation is already high,
- and the choice is between “slightly more money” vs “much better day-to-day work,”

the literature usually points toward **job content** mattering more for sustained satisfaction than the extra salary.

That is especially relevant in your case because this is presumably **already a high-income range**, where marginal utility from additional salary is typically lower than at lower income levels.

## 3) Meaningful work and value alignment are strong predictors

There is also a sizable literature on **meaningful work** and **person-job / person-organization fit**.

### Meaningful work
Meta-analytic evidence shows that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report higher:
- job satisfaction
- well-being
- engagement
and lower:
- turnover intentions

Reference:
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes.

### Fit
One of the strongest results in organizational psychology is that **fit matters a lot**.

Reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: meta-analysis showing person-job and person-organization fit are strongly related to job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions.

This is very relevant to your situation because “I always wanted to lead a small team” is not just an external perk. It may reflect a real **intrinsic preference**:
- mentoring
- influence
- building a culture
- owning outcomes through others

If so, then the choice is **not** merely:
- interesting work vs more money

It is closer to:
- **technical/research interest** vs **leadership/impact interest**

Research would say the winner is usually the one with better **fit to your motives**, not the one with higher pay.

## 4) Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness

Another highly relevant literature is **Self-Determination Theory**.

It says that well-being is strongly shaped by whether work satisfies basic psychological needs for:
- **autonomy** — having control/choice
- **competence** — feeling effective and growing
- **relatedness** — feeling connected to others

Research in work settings consistently shows that when jobs satisfy these needs, people report better:
- motivation
- vitality
- job satisfaction
- well-being

This matters because “interesting research” often strongly supports **competence** and sometimes autonomy, while “leading a small team” may add:
- autonomy/influence
- relatedness
- significance/impact

So again, the research does **not** imply “always choose the more intellectually interesting role.”  
It implies: choose the role that better satisfies your **intrinsic needs** on a daily basis.

## 5) Leadership/promotion: mixed effects, not automatically better

The evidence on promotion/leadership is more mixed than the evidence on job design.

Typical findings in panel/longitudinal work:
- promotions can increase satisfaction somewhat
- but often also increase:
  - stress
  - workload
  - time pressure
  - role conflict
- and the satisfaction gain can be **smaller or shorter-lived** than people expect

This is especially true when the promoted role changes the nature of work in a way the person does not actually enjoy.

That matters because many people say they want “leadership,” but what they really want is:
- more influence
- more ownership
- more scope

Those are not identical to spending much more time on:
- hiring
- performance management
- coordination
- communication
- politics
- process

So the research-based question is not “is leadership better?”  
It is “does **this** leadership role improve or worsen my daily fit?”

## 6) What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

There is **much less direct research** on:
- “People who chose the more interesting job—did they regret it later?”
than on:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- well-being
- turnover

What the adjacent evidence suggests is:

### Stronger evidence
People tend to do better over time when their work aligns with:
- intrinsic interests
- values
- psychological needs
- person-job fit

### Weaker / indirect evidence
Research on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations suggests that prioritizing intrinsic goals (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) is generally associated with better well-being than prioritizing extrinsic rewards (money, status, image) alone.

That doesn’t prove “you’ll regret the money choice.”  
But it does suggest that **sacrificing a lot of intrinsic fit for a moderate extrinsic gain is often not a great trade**.

So if you want the most research-defensible statement, it would be:

> There is strong evidence that more intrinsically rewarding work predicts greater job satisfaction and well-being than modest pay gains do, but there is less direct evidence on retrospective regret from this exact tradeoff.

## 7) If I translate the literature into your specific decision

A research-grounded reading of your case would be:

- **If the Anthropic offer is mainly “a bit more money” and “less interesting work,”** the literature would generally predict **lower day-to-day satisfaction** than staying in a more interesting role.
- **If the Anthropic offer fulfills a major intrinsic aspiration—leading/mentoring/building—and you care deeply about that**, then it may still improve your long-run satisfaction despite the work being less interesting technically.
- The extra salary by itself is probably the **least important variable** here, unless the difference is large enough to meaningfully change your life outside work.

So the empirical core is:

### Most likely stronger predictors of your future happiness
1. How much you enjoy the **actual daily work**
2. Whether the role matches your **core motives**  
   - research/creation/discovery  
   - or leadership/mentoring/organizational leverage
3. Autonomy, meaning, and growth
4. Manager-role fit

### Likely weaker predictor
5. A modest salary increase

## 8) Best-supported bottom line

If you want the cleanest evidence-based conclusion:

> **Yes: people in more interesting, meaningful, autonomous, well-fitting jobs tend to be happier at work, and usually more satisfied overall, than people who move to slightly higher-paying but less intrinsically rewarding jobs.**  
> The main exception is when the “less interesting” job satisfies another major intrinsic motive—such as leadership, mentoring, or influence—that fits the person very well.

## References worth looking up

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried, Y., & Ferris, G. R. (1987)** — meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-job / person-organization fit
- **Allan, B. A., Batz-Barbarich, C., Sterling, H. M., & Tay, L. (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work outcomes

If you want, I can also give you a **more academic summary with effect sizes and a “what this implies for your exact tradeoff” table**, sticking only to published findings.

answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

- **Interesting/intrinsically motivating work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
- **It usually predicts satisfaction and engagement more strongly than salary level alone does.**
- **But it is not the only thing that matters**: autonomy, manager quality, workload, fairness, fit with your values, and growth opportunities are also major drivers.
- On **regret**, there is much less direct evidence specifically on “choosing the more interesting job vs. the better-paid less interesting one.” The literature is stronger on **later satisfaction, engagement, burnout, and turnover** than on retrospective regret.

A few robust findings:

## 1) “Interesting work” matters a lot

In the work-design literature, “interesting” is usually broken into things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **challenge/learning**
- **meaningfulness**

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses found these features are consistently associated with:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal motivation**
- higher **engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- often better **performance**

A particularly important meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, a meta-analysis of work design research.  
  Their overall conclusion was that motivational job characteristics—especially **autonomy**, **task significance**, and **variety**—have substantial positive relationships with satisfaction and motivation.

So if by “interesting” you mean “the work itself is intellectually engaging and feels meaningful,” the evidence says that is not a trivial preference; it is a major predictor of how good the job feels.

## 2) Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

There is also a separate literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful review/meta-analysis here is:

- **Allan et al. (2019)** on outcomes of meaningful work.

The broad pattern is that people who experience their work as meaningful report more:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- life meaning
- commitment

and less:

- turnover intention

That doesn’t mean “meaning” always beats all other factors. But it does mean that if one job feels substantially more meaningful or intrinsically engaging, that is likely to matter to day-to-day well-being.

## 3) Pay matters, but less than many people expect

The research on salary and job satisfaction is pretty consistent: the link exists, but it is **modest**.

A standard citation is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

Their conclusion: **higher pay is associated with higher job satisfaction, but the relationship is relatively small-to-moderate**, not huge.

That is one of the more important findings for your question. It means:

- salary is **not irrelevant**
- but salary differences often do **less** for long-run satisfaction than factors like interesting work, autonomy, fit, and manager/team quality

This is especially true when the salary difference is “a bit higher” rather than “financially life-changing.”

## 4) Fit matters: values and role fit are strong predictors

Another important body of research is on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**.

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

They found that better fit is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher commitment
- lower intention to quit
- better well-being-related outcomes

This matters for your case because your tradeoff is not simply:

- interesting work vs more money

It is also:

- IC/research depth vs team leadership
- current mission/environment vs a different culture
- present fit vs aspirational fit

If “leading a small team” is something you have wanted for a long time, that itself is a meaningful part of fit. The research would say that **alignment between the role and your values/identity** matters a lot.

## 5) Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are repeatedly important

The **Self-Determination Theory** literature is also relevant. Across many studies, jobs that support:

- **autonomy** (“I have real control”)
- **competence** (“I’m growing / using my strengths”)
- **relatedness** (“I feel connected and respected”)

are associated with better motivation and well-being.

A review often cited in workplace contexts is:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
and later reviews/meta-analytic work on basic psychological needs at work.

This implies something important for your specific situation:

A move into leadership might increase **autonomy**, **competence development**, and **growth**, even if the technical content is less interesting. So the tradeoff is not just “interestingness” vs “money”; it may be:

- **technical intrinsic interest** in one job
versus
- **leadership growth/autonomy/identity fulfillment** in the other

Research would treat both as psychologically meaningful.

## 6) Does staying in the more interesting job make people happier overall?

Usually, yes—**if** the interest comes with decent levels of autonomy, fairness, and manageable stress.

Why the qualification? Because interesting work can be undermined by:

- chronic overload
- bad management
- politics
- low fairness
- poor work-life boundaries

Job satisfaction also spills over into broader well-being. There is a long literature showing **moderate links between job satisfaction and life satisfaction**, generally interpreted as partly reciprocal.

So, yes, more interesting work tends to be associated with people being happier—not just at work—but that effect can be weakened or reversed if the environment is bad enough.

## 7) What about regret?

This is where the research is weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong body of evidence specifically asking:

> Do people regret choosing the more interesting job over the higher-paid less interesting one?

What the literature does support more confidently is:

- people are more satisfied over time when their work is **intrinsically motivating**
- people are more satisfied when the role fits their values and identity
- purely extrinsic factors like pay/status are often **overweighted in advance** relative to their long-run contribution to daily well-being

There is also a broader regret literature suggesting that, over long horizons, people often regret **not pursuing important values or aspirations**. But that literature is broad and not specific enough to use as a strong answer to your exact question.

So the evidence-based answer is:

- **strong evidence on satisfaction**
- **weaker and more indirect evidence on regret**

## The most research-consistent way to think about your decision

The literature would suggest you should not frame this as only:

- **interesting work** vs **higher salary**

Instead, decompose it into the main predictors of well-being:

1. **Intrinsic interest / meaningfulness**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Growth / mastery**
4. **Fit with your identity and values**
5. **Manager/team quality**
6. **Workload and sustainability**
7. **Compensation / fairness**
8. **Leadership aspiration fulfillment**

Then ask which option better satisfies the full set.

For example, if Anthropic is:
- less technically interesting,
- but gives you real leadership responsibility,
- more autonomy,
- stronger growth in a direction you deeply want,

then the evidence does **not** say “stay with the more interesting work no matter what.” It says total well-being depends on the whole motivational package.

But if the new role is:
- somewhat more money,
- a leadership title,
- yet genuinely less meaningful day to day,
- and likely worse fit or less intellectual engagement,

then the evidence would lean toward expecting **lower day-to-day job satisfaction**, even if the move looks better on paper.

---

## If you want the shortest evidence-based summary

- **Interesting, meaningful, intrinsically motivating work tends to predict higher job satisfaction and engagement than salary differences do.**
- **Pay helps, but usually not as much as people think.**
- **Leadership opportunities can also increase well-being if they satisfy a real aspiration for autonomy, growth, and identity fit.**
- **There is much stronger research on satisfaction than on regret.**

## Good references to start with

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis of work design  
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction  
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis of person-environment fit  
- **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis of meaningful work outcomes  
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — self-determination theory and work motivation review

If you want, I can give you a **more literature-style summary with effect sizes and citations**, or help you turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the research variables above**.

answer:
Yes. The literature is fairly consistent on one big point:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, learning, good colleagues, fit with your motives — tend to matter **a lot** for job satisfaction and well-being, often **more than modest differences in pay**.

A few research-backed takeaways:

## 1) “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction
Classic work-design research has found that jobs high in:

- **skill variety**
- **autonomy**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **challenge / learning**

produce higher **internal motivation** and **job satisfaction**.

Key references:
- **Hackman & Oldham (1976)** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Fried & Ferris (1987)** — meta-analysis
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — large meta-analysis of work design

The 2007 meta-analysis is especially relevant: motivational job characteristics like autonomy, task variety, and significance showed **moderate-to-strong associations** with job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout/turnover intentions.

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, hard, learning-rich, meaningful,” then on average **yes, people in those jobs are usually happier with the job**.

## 2) Actual salary matters, but its link to job satisfaction is usually much smaller
There’s a well-known result in org psych / labor econ:

- **Actual pay level** has a **positive but fairly small** relationship with job satisfaction.
- Roughly speaking, the correlation is often in the **small** range compared with intrinsic job characteristics.

Key reference:
- **Judge et al. (2010)**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction

The usual interpretation is:
- pay matters a lot for **attraction**, fairness, security, and comparison to alternatives
- but **day-to-day satisfaction** depends more on what the work feels like

So a “bit higher salary” often does **less** for happiness than a big difference in how interesting/autonomous the work is.

## 3) Meaningful work predicts well-being and lower turnover intentions
There’s also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic evidence suggests that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report more:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- overall well-being

and less:
- turnover intention
- burnout / distress

Key reference:
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

This is relevant because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often travel together in research settings.

## 4) Person-job fit matters a lot — and may be the key variable in your case
A strong result in the literature is that **fit** matters a great deal:
- fit between your preferences/values and the job
- fit between your strengths and the role
- fit between your motives and the work structure

Key reference:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on fit

Why this matters for your case:
this is **not just** “interesting work vs more money.”

It’s also:
- **intellectual interest / research excitement**
vs
- **leadership opportunity**, which you say you’ve always wanted

From the literature’s perspective, both of those are **intrinsic** job features.  
So if leading a team is a deep personal motive, that can be a very important predictor of satisfaction too.

In other words, the evidence does **not** say “always choose the more interesting technical work.”  
It says: **choose the role with the stronger intrinsic fit**, because that usually predicts satisfaction better than a moderate pay bump.

## 5) There is direct evidence that scientists often accept lower pay for more interesting/autonomous work
This is especially relevant to research careers.

Economists studying scientists have found evidence of **compensating differentials**:
people are willing to accept lower pay in exchange for things like:
- intellectual freedom
- ability to publish
- scientific autonomy
- more authentic research work

Key references:
- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**
- **Sauermann & Roach** — work on scientists’ preferences for independence, challenge, salary, publishing, etc.

So in “revealed preference” terms, many scientists really do trade money for more intrinsically rewarding work.

## 6) On regret specifically: the evidence is weaker
This is the part where the literature is thinner.

There is **much stronger evidence on satisfaction/well-being** than on “career regret after choosing the less/more interesting job.”

A few things we do know:
- Broader regret research suggests that over the long run, people often regret **inaction** more than action.
  - **Gilovich & Medvec (1995)** is the classic reference.
- Research on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations generally finds better well-being when people pursue **intrinsic** goals (growth, relationships, contribution, mastery) rather than mainly **extrinsic** ones (money, status, image).
  - This is more indirect, but relevant.

What I would **not** say, based on the literature, is:
- “People who stay in the more interesting job usually regret it.”
I don’t know of strong evidence for that.

If anything, the literature leans the other way:
- sacrificing intrinsic quality for moderate extrinsic gain often does **not** maximize satisfaction

But again, regret-specific evidence is weaker than happiness/satisfaction evidence.

## 7) Important caveat: interesting work can also increase overwork
There’s a real counterpoint in the literature:

Work that is meaningful or identity-relevant can make people:
- tolerate worse conditions
- work too much
- blur boundaries
- accept stress because the work feels important

This shows up in research on **calling**, passion, and mission-driven work.

So “interesting” is not automatically better if it comes with:
- unsustainable hours
- chronic stress
- low control
- bad management

The best outcomes are usually from work that is:
- interesting
- autonomous
- well-supported
- sustainable
