answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research relevant to this, though not much on the exact choice “more interesting IC role vs less interesting manager role with slightly higher pay.” The best evidence comes from adjacent literatures in organizational psychology, labor economics, and well-being research.

## Short version

Across many studies, **intrinsic features of work** — especially **interest, autonomy, challenge, skill use, learning, and meaning** — tend to predict **job satisfaction and day-to-day well-being more strongly** than **pay level alone**, particularly once pay is already “good enough” and perceived as fair.

So, on average:

- **More interesting work** is usually associated with **higher job satisfaction, engagement, and lower burnout**.
- **Higher pay** does improve satisfaction and life evaluation, but the effect is usually **smaller than people expect**, and often smaller than the effect of the work itself.
- **Leadership opportunities** can raise satisfaction **if leading is intrinsically important to the person**, but management roles also often bring more stress, admin burden, and less direct task enjoyment.

On **regret** specifically: there is **less direct evidence**, but the broader literature suggests people tend to do better long-term when choices align with **intrinsic motivation and personal values**, not just external rewards.

---

## What “interesting work” usually means in research

Researchers usually don’t measure “interesting” as a single thing. They break it into constructs like:

- **Autonomy** — control over how you work
- **Skill variety / challenge** — using and growing capabilities
- **Task significance / meaning** — feeling the work matters
- **Feedback / mastery** — knowing if you’re getting better
- **Intrinsic motivation** — doing the work because it is engaging in itself

So when I say “interesting work,” I mostly mean that cluster.

---

## 1) Job design and intrinsic work quality are strong predictors of satisfaction

One of the most robust findings in work psychology is that the design of the job itself matters a lot.

### Classic framework
**Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** argued that jobs high in:
- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

produce more internal motivation and job satisfaction.

### Meta-analytic evidence
A major meta-analysis:
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**

found that these kinds of motivational job characteristics are substantially associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **internal work motivation**
- better **subjective well-being**
- lower **strain/burnout**

The broad takeaway from that literature is very stable:  
**what you actually do all day matters a lot for how good work feels.**

---

## 2) Pay matters, but usually less than intrinsic job quality

There is definitely research on pay and job satisfaction.

### Meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
A commonly cited one is:
- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**

Their conclusion was basically:
- the relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **real**
- but it is **modest**, not huge

A useful summary is:
- pay matters
- but it is far from the dominant driver of job satisfaction

In practical terms, the link between pay and satisfaction is usually **smaller** than the link between satisfaction and things like:
- autonomy
- meaningfulness
- interesting/challenging work
- good management
- fit with values

### Why this happens
A few reasons recur in the literature:

1. **Adaptation**: people often get used to higher compensation.
2. **Relative pay matters**: fairness and comparison to peers often matter as much as absolute pay.
3. **Daily experience dominates**: your work content affects you every day; salary is often more background unless it changes your life materially.

Related evidence:
- **Card et al. (2012)** showed that peer salary comparisons affect job satisfaction; fairness matters a lot.
- In broader well-being research, income improves life evaluation, but the marginal gain tends to shrink relative to other quality-of-life factors, especially at higher income levels.

---

## 3) Meaningful / engaging work predicts happiness and lower burnout

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work** and **intrinsic motivation**.

### Self-Determination Theory
A major theoretical and empirical line, from **Deci & Ryan**, finds that people tend to do better when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

When those needs are met, people usually report:
- more well-being
- more energy
- better motivation
- less burnout

This is strongly relevant to “interesting work,” because interesting work often supports autonomy and competence.

### Meaningful work research
Meta-analyses and reviews on meaningful work generally find that meaningfulness is associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **engagement**
- better **general well-being**
- lower **turnover intentions**

So if by “interesting” you mean “I actually care about the substance of the work,” the literature generally treats that as a major positive.

---

## 4) Intrinsic motives usually outperform extrinsic motives for long-term well-being

Another relevant body of research compares **intrinsic** vs **extrinsic** goals.

Very broadly:
- **intrinsic goals**: growth, mastery, contribution, relationships
- **extrinsic goals**: money, status, image

Across many studies, people whose choices are more aligned with intrinsic motives tend to report:
- higher well-being
- more satisfaction
- better persistence
- less inner conflict

This does **not** mean money is bad. It means that when someone chooses mainly based on external rewards, that often predicts less durable satisfaction than choices aligned with genuine interest and values.

That is probably the closest research answer to your “do they regret it?” question:  
there is decent evidence that **intrinsically aligned choices age better psychologically** than choices made mainly for external rewards.

---

## 5) Leadership can help — but only if leadership itself is intrinsically attractive to you

This is the major complication in your case.

You are not comparing only:
- interesting work
vs
- higher salary

You’re also comparing:
- individual contributor work
vs
- leading a small team

And leadership is its own motivational domain.

### Relevant research
There is a literature on **motivation to lead** (e.g. **Chan & Drasgow, 2001**). It suggests that people differ a lot in whether leadership is energizing or draining.

If someone has strong **affective motivation to lead** — meaning they actually want the role, not just the status — leadership tends to be a better fit.

### Why this matters
Management roles often increase:
- responsibility
- social impact
- autonomy
- influence

But they also often increase:
- meetings
- conflict management
- people problems
- administrative overhead
- context switching
- less time on the core craft

So research would not say “leadership is better” or “IC is better” in general. It would say:

> leadership improves satisfaction mainly when the person genuinely wants the leadership work itself, not just the idea of advancement.

That is especially relevant because you said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.”  
From a research perspective, that is not a trivial perk; it is evidence that leadership may itself be an **intrinsic motivator** for you.

---

## 6) On regret: direct evidence is thinner than on satisfaction

This part is important.

I don’t know of a strong literature that directly tracks people who faced the exact tradeoff:
- “stay in more interesting work”
vs
- “take slightly more money and leadership in less interesting work”

and then measures long-term regret.

What we do have are indirect literatures:

### A) Affective forecasting
People often **overestimate** how much long-term happiness they’ll get from salient external changes like:
- compensation
- title
- prestige

and **underestimate** how much repeated daily experience matters.

That would predict that people may overvalue salary/title at decision time.

### B) Intrinsic vs extrinsic goal pursuit
As above, choices aligned with intrinsic goals generally produce more durable well-being.

### C) Person-job fit
Regret is more likely when there is poor fit between:
- one’s motives and strengths
- the actual content of the role

So the best evidence-based answer on regret is:

> direct regret evidence is limited, but the available psychology suggests that long-term satisfaction is more likely when the choice fits intrinsic interests and values, especially the work you will actually be doing day to day.

---

## 7) What the literature would probably predict in a case like yours

Very cautiously, and averaging across studies:

If the salary bump is only **moderate**, and your current job is substantially more interesting, the research would generally predict that **staying in the more intrinsically rewarding work** is more likely to maximize:
- day-to-day enjoyment
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- lower risk of burnout/regret

**unless** one of these is true:

1. **Leading people is itself deeply intrinsically meaningful to you**  
   Then the new role may not actually be “less intrinsically rewarding”; it may be rewarding in a different dimension.

2. **The pay difference materially changes life circumstances**  
   e.g. family plans, financial security, location, optionality.

3. **The current role’s interest comes with hidden costs**  
   e.g. bad culture, unsustainable hours, low advancement, little autonomy.

4. **The new role offers much more autonomy/impact despite less interesting domain content**  
   Sometimes autonomy and influence can compensate for less exciting subject matter.

---

## 8) A concise evidence-based takeaway

If I compress the literature into one sentence:

> For people who are already well compensated, the strongest and most durable predictors of work happiness tend to be the intrinsic quality of the work and fit with one’s motives, not a modest increase in salary or status.

And a second sentence, specific to your case:

> But if “leading a small team” is a genuine intrinsic aspiration rather than just a résumé/status benefit, then leadership itself belongs on the “interesting/meaningful work” side of the ledger, not only on the “extrinsic reward” side.

---

## Some useful references to look at

If you want papers rather than summaries, these are good starting points:

1. **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976).**  
   *Motivation through the design of work.*  
   Classic job characteristics model.

2. **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   Strong summary of how work design affects satisfaction and well-being.

3. **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   Good reference for the “pay matters, but modestly” point.

4. **Deci, E. L., Ryan, R. M.** and broader **Self-Determination Theory** literature.  
   For autonomy, competence, intrinsic motivation, and well-being.

5. **Card, D., Mas, A., Moretti, E., & Saez, E. (2012).**  
   *Inequality at work: The effect of peer salaries on job satisfaction.*  
   Useful on pay fairness/comparison effects.

6. **Chan, K.-Y., & Drasgow, F. (2001).**  
   *Toward a theory of individual differences and leadership: Understanding the motivation to lead.*  
   Relevant for whether management will actually feel fulfilling.

---

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more academic literature review** with 10–15 papers and brief findings, or  
2. translate the research into a **decision framework** specifically for “interesting IC role vs higher-pay leadership role.”

It's a valid research point to highlight, but I'll avoid overly personal details. A final section labeled "Evidence-based takeaway" sounds like a solid plan!
**Considering job satisfaction factors**

I’m noticing that factors like intrinsic interests and satisfaction in roles might impact retention more than slight salary increases. It seems that moving into leadership can enhance well-being, but only if the management itself is meaningful. The biggest risk might be accepting a role that conflicts with daily motivation. While literature indicates these trends, outcomes aren't guaranteed. I’ll consider citing several studies that delve deeper into job satisfaction and fit.
**Citing psychological needs research**

It looks like I need to reference the 2016 piece by Van den Broeck et al. concerning self-determination theory's psychological needs at work, although it’s not a meta-analysis. I might also mention a systematic review on basic psychological need satisfaction and motivation. To solidify my points, I should cite earlier studies by Gagné & Deci as foundational, while acknowledging that subsequent research supports their findings. Plus, I could include insights on intrinsic vs. extrinsic aspirations and the relationship between promotions and job satisfaction.
**Creating a comparative table**

Promotion and leadership could show small to moderate associations but might also bring more stress, which is a mixed bag. I should clarify that "interesting work" isn’t a formal construct. This table could really help provide clarity!
**Drafting on motivation factors**

These concepts are interconnected and can really influence productivity and satisfaction in any task. Alright, I want to make sure I present these ideas clearly and concisely in my draft. My goal is to express these thoughts in a polished way without making it too wordy. Let’s see how this comes together!
answer:
Yes. The short version from organizational psychology and labor research is:

**“Interesting work” — usually operationalized as intrinsically motivating, meaningful, challenging, autonomy-rich, and a good fit — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.**  
**Pay matters, but typically much less than people expect once compensation is already “good enough” and perceived as fair.**

A few concrete findings:

## 1) Pay has a real but surprisingly modest link to job satisfaction
A widely cited meta-analysis found that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is only **small**.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**: meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction  
  - The association is positive, but **quite modest** (often summarized around **r ≈ .15**).

Interpretation: higher pay does help, but **it usually does not buy a large increase in job satisfaction by itself**.

This fits broader happiness research too: income improves well-being, especially when it changes security and constraints, but **marginal increases at already-high incomes generally have diminishing psychological returns**, especially compared with daily experience factors like whether your work is engaging or draining.

## 2) The “work itself” tends to matter more than pay
In classic job satisfaction research, one of the most important facets is **the work itself**: whether the tasks are interesting, varied, meaningful, and allow skill use.

The best-known framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), and later meta-analyses show that job features like:

- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **feedback**
- **job complexity / challenge**

have **moderate to strong** associations with:

- job satisfaction
- intrinsic motivation
- engagement
- lower turnover intentions

A good reference:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**: meta-analysis of work design  
  - Found that motivational job characteristics are **substantially related** to job satisfaction and internal motivation, generally **more strongly than pay is**.

This is probably the closest research analogue to your “interesting work” question.

## 3) Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being
There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**, which overlaps a lot with “interesting enough that I care.”

Meta-analytic work finds that meaningful work is linked to:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- greater **life meaning**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- lower burnout / distress

A useful reference:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay** (meta-analysis on meaningful work; often cited around 2019)

The effect sizes here are usually **moderate to fairly large**, and again tend to be larger than the pay–satisfaction link.

## 4) Intrinsic motivation tends to beat extrinsic motivation for well-being
A robust line of research from **Self-Determination Theory** says people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Work that is interesting and self-endorsed tends to support those needs. Work chosen mainly for extrinsic reasons like money or status tends to do less well on them.

Key sources:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** on self-determination at work
- broader SDT literature and later reviews/meta-analyses on need satisfaction at work

Relatedly, research on **intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations** finds that people who place more weight on **money/status/image** relative to **growth/connection/contribution** tend, on average, to report **lower well-being**, even when they attain the extrinsic goals.

- **Kasser & Ryan (1993, 1996)** are foundational here.

That doesn’t mean salary is unimportant. It means that **once salary is adequate, prioritizing extrinsic gains over intrinsically rewarding work often does not maximize happiness**.

## 5) Person-job fit matters a lot
Another strong finding: **fit** predicts satisfaction.

If a role fits your motives, strengths, identity, and preferred way of working, people tend to be more satisfied and less likely to want to leave.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**: meta-analysis on person-job and person-organization fit

This is relevant because “interesting” is partly about the job itself, but also about **whether this particular person finds this particular work engaging**.

So the research-supported question is not just “Is the work interesting?” but also:

- “Does this role fit what I care about?”
- “Will I enjoy the day-to-day?”
- “Does it support the kind of growth I actually want?”

## 6) What about leadership opportunities?
This part is more mixed.

There is research suggesting that **promotions** can increase job satisfaction somewhat, and not only because of pay. But the gains are often offset by:

- more stress
- longer hours
- less autonomy over one’s time in some contexts
- less time doing the technical work one actually enjoys

One paper often cited here is:

- **Kosteas (2011)** on promotions and job satisfaction

The important nuance for your case is:

If **leading a small team is itself intrinsically meaningful to you**, then it should not be treated as a purely extrinsic factor. It may satisfy autonomy, competence, impact, and identity-related motives. In that case, the research would not classify it as “money/status vs interesting work.” It may actually belong on the **intrinsic** side.

So the real tradeoff may be:

- **technical/research interest**
vs
- **leadership interest / growth / impact**

Both can be intrinsically rewarding.

## 7) Do people later regret choosing the more interesting job?
This is the part where the literature is thinner.

I’m not aware of a strong, clean body of evidence showing:

> “People who chose the more interesting but lower-paid job later regretted it less/more than those who took the higher-paid but less interesting one.”

The evidence is more indirect. What we do know is:

- low meaning, boredom, and poor fit predict dissatisfaction and turnover
- pay has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction
- intrinsic goal pursuit is generally associated with higher well-being than extrinsic goal pursuit
- management/promotion helps mainly when it aligns with what the person actually wants

So if you want the research-based answer, it is:

**There is strong evidence that more interesting / meaningful / autonomy-supportive work is associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being than higher pay alone.**  
**There is much less direct evidence on long-run regret specifically.**

## If you want the cleanest evidence-based summary
If I compress the literature into one sentence:

> **For people already above a reasonable compensation threshold, daily work quality — especially interest, meaning, autonomy, challenge, and fit — usually predicts happiness at work much more strongly than incremental pay does.**

And a second important sentence for your exact situation:

> **A leadership role can improve well-being if leading is itself an intrinsic aspiration; if it mainly pulls you away from work you find deeply engaging, the research would predict a mixed outcome rather than a clear gain.**

## A rough “importance ranking” from the literature
Very roughly, for job satisfaction / work well-being:

1. **Interesting/meaningful work**
2. **Autonomy**
3. **Fit with your motives and identity**
4. **Good colleagues / manager / social environment**
5. **Growth / mastery**
6. **Pay fairness and adequacy**
7. **Incremental pay above adequacy**

That’s a simplification, but it is much closer to the literature than the common intuition that salary dominates.

## Good starting references
If you want to actually read papers, these are good anchors:

- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010).** The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis.
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007).** Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary.
- **Kristof-Brown, A. L., Zimmerman, R. D., & Johnson, E. C. (2005).** Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis.
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005).** Self-determination theory and work motivation.
- **Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M. (1993, 1996).** Work on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations and well-being.
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay** on meaningful work and well-being.
- **Kosteas (2011)** on promotions and job satisfaction.

If you want, I can give you a **more decision-oriented summary strictly grounded in the literature**, e.g. a 2-column framework translating your specific tradeoff into variables the research actually measures.

answer:
Yes. The broad research answer is:

**Intrinsic job qualities** — how interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and growth-oriented the work is — are usually **stronger predictors of job satisfaction and work well-being than pay level**.  
**Direct evidence on “later regret” is much thinner**, but the closest literatures (person-job fit, intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation, meaningful work) point in the same direction: people tend to do better when their work aligns with their interests/values, and worse when they optimize mainly for external rewards.

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly tied to job satisfaction

In the literature, “interesting” is usually captured indirectly through constructs like:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **autonomy**
- **skill variety**
- **task significance / meaningfulness**
- **learning and challenge**
- **person-job fit**

### Classic result: Job design matters a lot
The **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham) and later meta-analyses found that jobs with more:

- autonomy
- skill variety
- task significance
- feedback

produce higher:

- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- often better performance

A major meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — work design meta-analysis

This is one of the strongest and most replicated findings in organizational psychology.

### Meaningful work is also a strong predictor
Meta-analytic work on **meaningful work** finds medium-to-large positive associations with:

- job satisfaction
- work engagement
- organizational commitment
- life satisfaction

and negative associations with:

- turnover intentions
- burnout-related outcomes

Example:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on outcomes of meaningful work

So if by “interesting” you mean “I genuinely care about the problems and find them mentally engaging,” research generally says that matters a lot.

---

## 2) Salary matters, but less than people usually expect

There is a real positive relationship between pay and job satisfaction — it’s not zero — but it is **modest**.

A widely cited meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction

The basic result is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but fairly small** by the standards of psychology/organizational research.

So:

- more pay tends to help
- but it explains much less of job satisfaction than many people intuitively think
- non-pay job attributes often matter more

This matches broader well-being research too: income tends to matter most for:

- reducing financial stress
- improving overall life evaluation

but less for the **day-to-day experience of the work itself**.

Especially once compensation is already high enough that basic and many comfort needs are met, the marginal well-being gain from “somewhat more salary” is typically smaller than the gain/loss from what you actually do all day.

---

## 3) Intrinsic motivation usually beats extrinsic motivation for well-being

A large literature from **Self-Determination Theory** finds that people do better when work satisfies:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

and when they are driven more by **autonomous/intrinsic motivation** than by controlled/extrinsic motives.

Key references:

- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — SDT at work review
- broad later reviews/meta-analytic work on need satisfaction at work

The pattern is consistent:

- intrinsic/autonomous motivation predicts higher satisfaction, persistence, engagement, and often creativity
- extrinsic motives are not useless, but they are weaker predictors of well-being
- in some contexts, strong external rewards can even undermine intrinsic motivation

That does **not** mean “money is bad.”  
It means that, for long-run satisfaction, *why* you are doing the work and *what the work feels like* usually matter more than the external reward alone.

---

## 4) Person-job fit predicts satisfaction and lower turnover

Another useful literature is **person-job fit / person-organization fit**.

People are more satisfied, more committed, and less likely to want to leave when their job matches:

- their interests
- their values
- their strengths
- their preferred way of working

A classic meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

This is relevant because “interestingness” is partly objective and partly about **fit**.  
A role can be prestigious or higher-paying and still feel wrong if the actual work content is less aligned with your interests.

---

## 5) In research/science careers, people often *do* trade pay for interesting work

This is especially relevant to your situation.

There is labor-economics research showing that scientists and researchers are often willing to accept lower pay in exchange for:

- research freedom
- interesting problems
- publication opportunities
- intellectual autonomy

A famous example:

- **Stern (2004), “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**

The basic finding is that many scientists implicitly accept lower wages to do the kind of work they find intrinsically valuable.

Related work by **Sauermann and Roach** on science PhDs also finds that many strongly value research content and autonomy, not just salary.

So there is not only self-report evidence; there is also **revealed-preference evidence** that intellectually interesting work carries substantial utility.

---

## 6) What about leadership/promotion?

This is more mixed.

Research on promotions and managerial roles generally finds:

- positives: more status, pay, influence, autonomy, growth
- negatives: more stress, coordination load, administrative work, role conflict, time pressure

So the well-being effect of moving into leadership is **heterogeneous**.

In other words:

- if someone genuinely wants to lead, it can raise satisfaction
- but leadership is not a universal upgrade in happiness
- it often improves some dimensions while worsening others

That is important because “less interesting work, but leading a team” is not a simple good-for-bad exchange in the literature. Leadership may increase autonomy/impact while decreasing intellectual absorption or day-to-day enjoyment.

I’m not aware of a clean body of evidence saying that “taking a team-lead role despite less interesting work” systematically produces more or less regret. The evidence is much more conditional on fit.

---

## 7) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is the part where the evidence is weakest.

I’m **not aware of a strong direct longitudinal literature** that asks, in effect:

> “People had a choice between a more interesting job and a less interesting but better-paid/promoted job — who regretted it later?”

What we *do* have are strong proxy literatures:

- **meaningful/interesting work** → higher satisfaction, lower turnover intention
- **better fit** → higher satisfaction, lower burnout
- **intrinsic motivation** → better well-being
- **pay** → positive but smaller effect on job satisfaction

So the indirect evidence points fairly clearly in one direction, but it is still indirect.

---

## The closest thing to a research consensus

If I had to compress the literature into one sentence:

> **People are usually happier in jobs that are intrinsically engaging, meaningful, and autonomy-supportive, and those factors often matter more for sustained job satisfaction than somewhat higher pay.**

And a second sentence:

> **There is much less direct evidence on regret, but the best available evidence suggests that misfit and loss of intrinsic motivation are bigger long-run risks than forgoing a moderate salary increase.**

---

## Good papers/reviews to start with

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge et al. (2010)** — meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction
- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)** — meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit
- **Gagné & Deci (2005)** — self-determination theory and work motivation
- **Stern (2004)** — *Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?*

If you want, I can turn this into a **more formal literature summary with a table of effect sizes and stronger/weaknesses of each paper**.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of research adjacent to your question, though not many studies on your exact tradeoff.

The short version from organizational psychology and labor economics is:

- **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
- **Pay matters, but usually less than people intuitively expect, especially once basic financial needs are comfortably met.**
- **Fit matters a lot**: if you genuinely want to lead, the chance to lead a team is not a minor detail; it is a separate predictor of satisfaction.
- **Direct research on “regret” is thinner** than research on job satisfaction, burnout, turnover, and well-being.

## 1) What research usually means by “interesting work”

Researchers usually don’t use “interesting” as the main technical term. They measure nearby constructs such as:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **task variety**
- **skill use**
- **autonomy**
- **challenge**
- **meaningfulness / purpose**
- **person-job fit**
- **interest congruence** (how well the work matches what you naturally like doing)

So the evidence is strongest for those constructs rather than the word “interesting” itself.

---

## 2) The strongest result: work itself matters a lot

A very robust finding is that the **design of the work** strongly affects satisfaction.

### Work design / job characteristics
The classic framework is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, later backed by large meta-analyses. The key ingredients are things like:

- autonomy
- task variety
- task significance
- task identity
- feedback

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*

found that these characteristics are **substantially associated** with:
- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower burnout/strain
- better performance

In practical terms, **autonomy and interesting/challenging task content tend to have much stronger links to job satisfaction than pay does**.

That’s probably the single closest literature to your question.

---

## 3) Meaningful work is consistently linked to higher satisfaction and well-being

There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**.

Meta-analytic work by **Allan and colleagues** and related reviews show that people who experience their work as meaningful tend to report:

- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- higher **life satisfaction / general well-being**
- lower **turnover intention**

The effects are usually **moderate to strong by social-science standards**, not trivial.

So if by “interesting” you partly mean “I’m intellectually engaged and the work feels significant,” the literature is pretty favorable to that.

### Important nuance
Meaningful/interesting work is **not always protective** if the environment is bad. If the role also brings:
- long hours
- low control
- poor management
- constant stress

then the benefits can shrink or reverse. People can love the work and still burn out.

---

## 4) Pay helps, but much less than most people think

This is one of the cleanest results.

### Pay and job satisfaction
A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**

found the correlation between **pay level and job satisfaction** is **positive but small** — around **r ≈ .15**.

That means:
- salary matters
- but it explains **far less** of job satisfaction than many people assume

Put plainly: **a somewhat higher salary often does not buy much extra happiness from the job itself**.

### Income and overall well-being
In broader well-being research:
- higher income tends to improve **life evaluation**
- but its effects on **day-to-day emotional experience** are smaller and often show diminishing returns

The exact shape is debated, but the overall conclusion is stable:
**once you are not financially strained, non-monetary job features often matter more for daily lived experience.**

So if the Anthropic offer is “a bit higher salary,” the literature would generally treat that as a meaningful factor, but **not usually the dominant one**, unless it materially changes your financial security or options.

---

## 5) Person-job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction

Another very strong line of evidence is **fit**.

### Person-job / person-organization fit
A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**

shows that fit between the person and the role/organization is strongly associated with:
- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower turnover intentions
- better well-being

This matters for your case because your choice is not just:
- interesting work vs more money

It is also:
- **individual-contributor research role** vs **leadership role**

If you have genuinely wanted to lead a small team for a long time, that may count as a **fit variable**, not a side detail.

Research generally suggests people are happier when their work fits:
- their interests
- their values
- their motives
- their preferred level of responsibility/influence

---

## 6) Interest congruence: people are happier when the work matches what they like

There is also research on **vocational interest congruence** — roughly, whether your work matches your natural interests.

That literature usually finds:
- the effect on satisfaction is **positive but modest**
- stronger when measured well

So people whose jobs align with what they actually enjoy tend to be more satisfied, though this effect is usually **not as large** as broader work design / meaning / fit effects.

This supports the general idea that **“interestingness” matters**, but suggests that “interesting” is strongest when it also comes with autonomy, challenge, and meaning.

---

## 7) What about leadership specifically?

Here the evidence is more nuanced.

There isn’t a simple universal result saying “people are happier as managers” or “people are happier as ICs.”

What the research suggests instead is:

- **Leadership can increase satisfaction** when it fits a person’s motives and identity
- It can **decrease satisfaction** when it pulls someone away from the kind of work they actually enjoy

This is consistent with:
- **person-role fit**
- **motivation-to-lead** research
- research showing many high performers are promoted into management roles that don’t match what they actually want to do day to day

So the literature would likely say:

> If “leading a small team” is a genuine long-term aspiration for you, that should be treated as a serious positive predictor of future satisfaction, not as a mere prestige/status add-on.

But:

> If the new role substantially reduces the part of work you find deeply engaging, that loss is also likely to matter a lot.

In other words, research does **not** reduce your choice to salary vs interest.  
It is more like:

- **daily task enjoyment / intrinsic motivation**
- **meaning**
- **fit with leadership aspirations**
- **salary**

And the first three often matter more than the last one.

---

## 8) Do people regret choosing the more interesting job?

This is where the evidence gets weaker.

I’m not aware of a strong literature that cleanly tracks:
- “took the more interesting job”
vs
- “took the better-paid, less interesting leadership job”

and then measures **regret** years later.

What researchers usually measure instead:
- job satisfaction
- life satisfaction
- burnout
- turnover
- commitment
- mental health
- career commitment

So the honest answer is:

- **Direct regret evidence is limited**
- But the best proxy outcomes generally favor:
  - meaningful/interesting work
  - good fit
  - autonomy
over
  - modest pay increases alone

There is also broader evidence from affective forecasting research suggesting that people often **overestimate the long-term happiness impact of money/status variables** relative to the quality of everyday experience.

That is not a perfect match to your question, but it points in the same direction.

---

## 9) The research-based synthesis most relevant to your situation

If I translate the literature into a compact summary:

### Strongest empirical pattern
People tend to be happier in jobs that provide:
- **interesting/challenging work**
- **autonomy**
- **meaning**
- **good fit with their interests and values**

### Salary
Higher salary improves things, but:
- its effect on job satisfaction is usually **small**
- especially when the increase is incremental rather than life-changing

### Leadership
A leadership opportunity can be a major positive **if you actually want it**
- because fit with desired responsibility matters
- but it does not automatically compensate for losing interesting work

So the research-based answer is something like:

> On average, people do tend to be happier in work that they find interesting/meaningful and that fits them well; moderate salary differences usually matter less than that.  
> However, if leading people is itself a major source of meaning/fit for you, that factor belongs in the same “intrinsic/job-quality” category rather than being treated as just an external perk.

---

## 10) If you want the most relevant papers to read

A good short reading list would be:

1. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**  
   *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature.*  
   -> Best source on how job characteristics like autonomy/variety/feedback affect satisfaction.

2. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature.*  
   -> Clean evidence that pay matters, but not nearly as much as many assume.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**  
   *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job, person-organization, person-group, and person-supervisor fit.*  
   -> Very relevant for your “IC researcher vs team lead” fit question.

4. **Hackman & Oldham (1976, 1980)**  
   Job Characteristics Model work  
   -> Classic framework for why certain jobs feel intrinsically motivating.

5. **Allan et al.** meta-analytic work on meaningful work  
   -> Meaningful work’s links to satisfaction, engagement, well-being, turnover.

6. For broader well-being / income:
   - **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
   - **Killingsworth (2021)**
   -> Income and happiness, with some disagreement on the exact curve but agreement that money is not the whole story.

---

If you want, I can do one of two more research-focused follow-ups:

1. give you a **more formal literature review** with citations and effect sizes, or  
2. help translate this into a **decision framework using only empirically supported factors**.

answer:
Yes — there is a fair amount of research relevant to this, though it usually doesn’t use the everyday label “interesting work.” The closest research constructs are:

- **intrinsic motivation**
- **meaningful work**
- **job characteristics** like variety, challenge, autonomy, feedback, skill use
- **person–job fit**

The short version of the literature is:

> **On average, people in work that is more intrinsically engaging, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to their skills/interests report higher job satisfaction, engagement, and somewhat higher overall well-being.**
>
> **Extra pay helps, especially when it changes security or perceived fairness, but beyond already-high income levels its marginal effect on happiness is usually smaller than people expect.**

A few more specific research-backed points:

---

## 1) “Interesting work” is strongly associated with job satisfaction

The classic framework here is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham). It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide:

- **skill variety**
- **task identity**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

Later meta-analytic work in organizational psychology — especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — found that these kinds of job features are **consistently and meaningfully related** to:

- job satisfaction
- internal work motivation
- engagement
- lower withdrawal/turnover intentions

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, varied, challenging, meaningful, and giving me room to exercise judgment,” then yes: **that kind of work tends to make people happier at work.**

---

## 2) Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

This matters because one could ask: “Maybe interesting work makes work nicer, but does it matter for life overall?”

Research says **yes, moderately**.

There is a longstanding literature showing a **moderate positive relationship** between **job satisfaction** and **life satisfaction**. A commonly cited ballpark is around **r ≈ .4** in meta-analytic summaries — not perfect, but substantial. Longitudinal work generally suggests the relationship goes **both ways**, but job quality does affect overall well-being.

So it’s not just “I’ll enjoy the workday more.” Since work occupies a large fraction of waking life, the day-to-day experience matters.

---

## 3) Intrinsic factors tend to predict well-being better than extrinsic ones once pay is “enough”

This is where **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) is useful. It emphasizes that people do better psychologically when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Jobs that satisfy those needs tend to produce more durable well-being than jobs that mainly offer extrinsic rewards like more pay or status.

Related research on **meaningful work** also finds that people who experience their work as meaningful report:

- higher job satisfaction
- greater engagement
- better well-being
- lower turnover intentions

This doesn’t mean money doesn’t matter. It means that **once compensation is already comfortably high**, the effect of a further increase is usually smaller than the effect of major differences in the daily work experience.

For someone already at a top AI lab, that caveat matters a lot.

---

## 4) People often adapt to salary/status changes more than they expect

The broader well-being literature shows a lot of **affective forecasting error**: people often overestimate how much a change in pay, prestige, or title will affect their long-run happiness.

On income specifically:

- Older work like **Kahneman & Deaton** suggested a plateau for emotional well-being.
- Newer work like **Killingsworth** suggests happiness may keep rising with income for many people, but **with diminishing returns**.

So the modern careful summary is:

> More money still helps, but the incremental happiness benefit of “a bit higher salary” is often modest, especially at already high income levels.

And importantly, salary increases are prone to adaptation.  
The structure of your workday — what you do for 8–10 hours — is repeated constantly, so it can matter a lot.

---

## 5) Leadership opportunity is also an intrinsic factor — not just an extrinsic perk

This is important in your case.

Your tradeoff is **not** simply:

- interesting work at OpenAI  
vs.
- more money at Anthropic

It’s really something more like:

- **more intellectually interesting individual-contributor work**
vs.
- **less interesting work but with a first real leadership role and a bit more pay**

Research would treat that leadership aspiration as potentially **intrinsic**, not just extrinsic. If leading a team is something you genuinely want, it may satisfy needs for:

- autonomy
- growth
- competence
- identity

So the evidence does **not** automatically imply “stay with the more interesting technical work.”

What the evidence says is more like:

> **The strongest predictors of satisfaction are usually intrinsic job qualities and fit.**
>
> In your case, both options contain intrinsic positives — just different ones.

That makes this less about “money vs meaning” and more about **which kind of intrinsic reward matters more to you right now**:
- technical fascination
- or leadership/growth

---

## 6) Promotions and management do not reliably make people happier

Research on promotions and managerial responsibility is mixed.

Leadership roles can improve well-being when they increase:

- autonomy
- influence
- sense of impact
- growth

But they can reduce well-being when they add:

- administrative burden
- interruptions
- people conflict
- role overload
- distance from the craft people actually enjoy

This is consistent with the **Job Demands–Resources** literature: more responsibility can be good if it comes with enough control/resources, and bad if it mainly means more demands.

So if you’re considering the Anthropic role, one of the key research-based questions is:

> **Would you actually be doing the kind of leading you’ve wanted, or mostly absorbing managerial friction?**

Those are very different jobs psychologically.

---

## 7) Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

Meta-analytic work on **person–environment fit** / **person–job fit** finds that fit is strongly linked to:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower intention to leave
- better well-being

That means the answer is not just “what do people generally prefer?” but:

- Does the OpenAI role fit your strongest motives now?
- Or has your motivation shifted toward building/leading, even if the technical content is somewhat less exciting?

The literature strongly supports the idea that **misfit is costly**, even in prestigious, high-paying roles.

---

## 8) On regret: direct evidence is thinner, but there are a couple useful findings

There isn’t a clean body of research saying “people who choose interesting jobs over better-paid jobs regret it less” in a simple way.

But two adjacent findings are relevant:

### a) Long-run regret often centers on missed opportunities / inaction
The classic regret work by **Gilovich & Medvec** suggests that over time, people often regret **the things they didn’t do** more than the things they did.

That doesn’t decide your case, but it means this question matters:

- If you stay, will you later feel you passed up your first real shot to lead?
- If you go, will you feel you left unusually interesting work too early?

### b) People underweight repeated daily experience
Well-being research suggests people often overweight headline attributes — salary, title, prestige — and underweight the emotional reality of ordinary days.

That argues for focusing less on the offer headline and more on:
- what the average Tuesday looks like in each role,
- what percentage of time is spent in energizing vs draining activities,
- and whether the work feels absorbing.

That’s very much supported by experience-based well-being research.

---

# So, what does the research imply in your specific setup?

A cautious, research-based read would be:

1. **If both jobs already pay very well**, the salary difference alone is unlikely to be the main determinant of long-run happiness.
2. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work usually predicts higher satisfaction** than people expect.
3. **But leadership opportunity can also be a powerful intrinsic good** if it matches a genuine aspiration.
4. The key variable is probably **fit**, not compensation:
   - If your strongest current motive is still “I want to work on the most interesting problems,” staying likely has a strong research-based case.
   - If your strongest current motive has become “I really want to learn to lead and build,” the Anthropic role may also have a strong research-based case, even if the content is less interesting.

So the literature does **not** support a blanket conclusion like “always choose the more interesting job,” but it **does** say that **intrinsic job quality should be weighted very heavily — usually more heavily than a modest pay bump.**

---

# If you want a practical research-based way to compare them

A good way to operationalize the literature is to score each role on these dimensions:

- **Autonomy**
- **Learning / skill growth**
- **Task variety**
- **Meaning / significance**
- **Day-to-day interest**
- **Fit with current identity**
- **Fit with future identity**
- **Managerial burden vs real leadership**
- **Burnout risk**
- **Compensation fairness/security**

If you want a single-sentence summary:

> **Research strongly favors intrinsic job quality over modest extrinsic gains, but in your case leadership itself is an intrinsic quality — so the real question is whether you value “interesting problems” or “leading people” more at this stage.**

If you want, I can also give you:
1. a **short reading list** of the most relevant papers/meta-analyses, or  
2. a **decision rubric** based directly on the research.

answer:
Yes—there is a fairly strong research literature on this, though it usually studies **“interesting work”** under labels like **intrinsic motivation, meaningful work, autonomy, skill variety, task significance, and person-job fit**.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology and labor economics, the broad pattern is:

1. **Intrinsic aspects of work** — interestingness, autonomy, meaning, learning, fit — are **strong predictors of job satisfaction and well-being**.
2. **Pay matters**, but **actual pay level is usually a much weaker predictor of overall job satisfaction** than those intrinsic factors, especially once pay is already high enough that basic financial stress is low.
3. **Promotions / leadership opportunities** can improve satisfaction for some people, but the benefit is mixed and depends a lot on whether the role fits what they actually enjoy doing.
4. On **regret specifically**, there is less direct evidence, but the closest literatures suggest that **value-fit and intrinsic motivation predict longer-term satisfaction better than salary differences do**.

So if your choice is really “more interesting work” versus “a bit more salary,” the research tends to favor the **interesting work** side for long-run satisfaction.  
If the real choice is “more interesting work” versus “a leadership role I deeply want,” then the literature says it depends much more on whether **leading** is itself intrinsically meaningful to you.

---

## What the research says

### 1) Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work predicts job satisfaction quite strongly

A classic line of work is the **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham), later supported by meta-analyses.

Core features associated with higher job satisfaction include:
- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**
- **opportunities to learn/grow**

A major meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis of the work-design literature. They found that motivational work characteristics like autonomy, variety, significance, and feedback were **moderately to strongly related** to outcomes like **job satisfaction**, **internal motivation**, and **engagement**.

The rough takeaway from this literature is that the day-to-day texture of the job matters a lot. Jobs people experience as rich, challenging, and meaningful are reliably linked to greater satisfaction.

---

### 2) Pay has a real effect, but it is much smaller than many people expect

There is a direct meta-analysis on **pay and job satisfaction**:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*  
  They found that the relationship between **actual pay level** and **overall job satisfaction** is **positive but small**.

This is one of the most relevant findings for your question. In plain English:

- More money usually helps somewhat.
- But how much people like their job is **not explained very well by salary alone**.
- Once someone is already well-paid, differences in salary tend to have **less impact** on satisfaction than differences in the quality of the work itself.

A related distinction that matters:
- **Pay level** has a smallish effect.
- **Pay satisfaction / perceived fairness of pay** can matter more.

So if the Anthropic offer mainly means “a bit more money,” research suggests that alone probably won’t dominate the effect of the job being less interesting. If instead it corrects a strong sense of underpayment or unfairness, it could matter more.

---

### 3) Meaningful work has robust links to both job satisfaction and broader well-being

There is also a substantial literature on **meaningful work**:

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**, meta-analysis on meaningful work  
  Meaningful work was associated with **higher job satisfaction**, **higher life satisfaction**, **greater work engagement**, and **lower distress/burnout**.

This matters because “interesting” often overlaps with:
- feeling that the work matters,
- feeling mentally engaged,
- using your strengths,
- seeing progress.

Those features are repeatedly associated with better outcomes than purely extrinsic rewards.

---

### 4) Person-job fit is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction

Another important literature is **person-environment fit**, especially **person-job fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)**, *Personnel Psychology*  
  Meta-analysis showing that fit with one’s job and organization is strongly associated with **job satisfaction**, **organizational commitment**, and lower **turnover intentions**.

This is relevant because some people genuinely care much more than average about:
- intellectual challenge,
- mission,
- technical depth,
- leadership,
- prestige,
- compensation,
- work-life balance, etc.

Research consistently suggests that the “right” choice depends a lot on whether the job matches what **you** care about most. A less interesting job can still be satisfying if it delivers on a personally central value like leadership or building an organization.

So the literature would not say “interesting work always wins.”  
It would say: **the better the fit with your intrinsic values, the happier you’re likely to be**.

---

### 5) Intrinsic motives tend to be better for long-term well-being than extrinsic motives

This is a big theme in **Self-Determination Theory** and adjacent research.

- **Deci & Ryan** and many later studies  
  Psychological well-being is strongly linked to satisfaction of the needs for:
  - **autonomy**
  - **competence**
  - **relatedness**

Jobs that support those needs tend to produce more durable well-being and motivation than jobs chosen mainly for extrinsic rewards like money or status.

A related pattern across many studies:
- People oriented more toward **intrinsic goals** tend to report better well-being than people oriented mainly toward **extrinsic goals** such as money/image/status, controlling for other things.

That doesn’t mean money/status don’t matter.  
It means they are usually **less reliable sources of durable satisfaction** than autonomy, mastery, meaning, and relationships.

---

### 6) Job satisfaction spills over into overall happiness

This is important because sometimes people say “I don’t need to love my job as long as life is good overall.” But research shows these aren’t fully separate.

- There is a long literature showing a **moderate positive relationship** between **job satisfaction** and **life satisfaction**.

So choosing a job that is more satisfying is not just about “being happier at work”; it often affects broader well-being too.

---

### 7) Economics also supports the idea that non-pay job quality is valuable

Labor economists study this via **compensating differentials**: unpleasant or less desirable jobs often have to pay more to attract workers.

That is not direct evidence on happiness, but it is evidence that workers place real value on non-monetary job attributes such as:
- interestingness,
- flexibility,
- autonomy,
- safety,
- schedule control,
- mission.

So if one role is “less interesting but higher paid,” economic theory would say that makes sense: the pay increase may literally be compensating for lower job quality.

---

## What about regret?

This is the weakest part of the evidence base.

There is **much less direct research** that asks something like:

> “Do people later regret choosing the higher-paid but less interesting job?”

I don’t know of a clean, canonical result that answers that exact question directly.

But the closest literatures suggest:

1. **Value-congruent choices** predict better long-term satisfaction.
2. People often **overestimate the long-term happiness effect of salary/status**.
3. People often **underestimate the cumulative effect of daily experience**.

That third point connects to the **focusing illusion** literature:

- **Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006)** and related work  
  People often overweight salient differences like income when forecasting happiness.

Applied cautiously to careers: when imagining a new job, people may focus heavily on salary/title and underweight what repeated days in that role will actually feel like.

So while I can’t say “the regret literature definitively proves X,” the evidence does suggest that people are often not great at forecasting how much the **day-to-day content** of work will matter relative to salary/status.

---

## What about leading a small team?

This is important, because your choice is not only “interesting work vs more money.”

It is also:
- **IC research role**
vs
- **leadership opportunity**

Research would treat that as a different kind of tradeoff.

Leading a team can increase:
- autonomy
- impact
- status
- growth
- sense of identity
- meaning

But it can also decrease:
- hands-on technical work
- flow
- deep work
- direct craft enjoyment

The literature on promotions/advancement generally finds:
- promotions can raise job satisfaction,
- but the effects are often **mixed** and depend heavily on fit,
- and they can come with higher stress, longer hours, and administrative load.

So from a research perspective, the leadership component is not just an extrinsic perk. It may be an intrinsic benefit **if** managing people is genuinely something you find meaningful.

That means the evidence-based comparison is probably not:

- interesting work vs money

but more like:

- **interesting technical work**
vs
- **leadership/impact/growth + more money + less interesting content**

That is a more balanced tradeoff.

---

## If you want the most evidence-based summary possible

Here is the strongest concise summary I can give:

### What predicts happiness/satisfaction at work most reliably?
Usually:
1. **Interesting/engaging work**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Meaning/significance**
4. **Fit with your values and strengths**
5. **Good relationships / manager / culture**
6. **Fair pay**
7. **Absolute pay level**

That ordering is not exact or universal, but it is closer to the research than the folk belief that salary dominates.

---

## So, to your question directly

> Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?

**On average, yes**—if “more interesting” also means more intrinsically motivating, meaningful, autonomous, and better-fitting. Those factors are consistently linked to higher job satisfaction and often to better overall well-being.

> Or do they regret that?

There is **less direct evidence on regret**, but the nearest evidence suggests that **people are more likely to be satisfied long term when their work fits their intrinsic values**, and that salary differences alone are often less important than people forecast.

---

## Papers / lines of work worth reading

If you want the most relevant starting points:

1. **Judge et al. (2010)** — *The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature*  
   Directly relevant for salary vs satisfaction.

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — *Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features*  
   Strong summary of how work characteristics affect satisfaction and motivation.

3. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — *Consequences of individuals’ fit at work: A meta-analysis of person-job/person-organization fit*  
   Useful for thinking about value fit.

4. **Allan et al. (2019)** — meta-analysis on **meaningful work**  
   Good for the “interesting/meaningful work” side.

5. **Deci & Ryan / Self-Determination Theory**  
   Best framework for why autonomy, competence, and relatedness often beat extrinsic rewards in long-term well-being.

6. **Kahneman et al. (2006)** — focusing illusion / forecasting happiness  
   Relevant for why salary/title can be overweighted in prediction.

---

If you want, I can do a second pass that is even more concrete:  
I can turn this into a **decision matrix grounded in the literature**—e.g. score each option on autonomy, mastery, meaning, leadership identity, compensation, fairness, and fit—and keep it strictly research-based.

answer:
Yes. The short version from the literature is:

- **“Interesting work”**—usually operationalized as some mix of **autonomy, variety, challenge, learning, meaning, and fit**—is one of the strongest and most consistent predictors of **job satisfaction** and often of broader well-being.
- **Pay matters**, but its relationship to job satisfaction is usually **much weaker** than people intuitively expect, especially once compensation is already high.
- **Leadership/management** can raise satisfaction for people who genuinely want it, but it also tends to bring **more stress, longer hours, and more role conflict**. The evidence here is more mixed and more person-dependent.
- On **regret specifically**, the evidence is thinner than for happiness/satisfaction, but the broader literature on **intrinsic vs. extrinsic goals** and **hedonic adaptation** suggests people often **overweight salary/status** and **underweight the daily experience of the work itself**.

A more careful research summary:

---

## 1) How important is “interesting work”?

In organizational psychology, “interesting work” is not usually one variable; it is decomposed into things like:

- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Autonomy**
- **Feedback**
- **Opportunities for learning / mastery**
- **Person-job fit**

These job characteristics are repeatedly found to be strongly related to satisfaction and motivation.

### Classic work-design research
The big foundational model is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. Their claim was that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they offer variety, identity, significance, autonomy, and feedback.

That model has been supported and extended by later reviews and meta-analyses. One of the most cited is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, meta-analysis on work design

Their overall result: “motivational” job characteristics—especially **autonomy**, **variety**, and **significance**—show substantial positive associations with:
- **job satisfaction**
- **internal motivation**
- **engagement**
- and lower **turnover intentions**

The main takeaway is not subtle: **the content of the work matters a lot**.

### Self-determination theory
A separate but convergent literature is **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan; work applications by Gagné, Van den Broeck, etc.). It finds that well-being at work is especially high when work satisfies three basic psychological needs:

- **Autonomy** — I have meaningful control
- **Competence** — I’m growing / using my abilities well
- **Relatedness** — I feel connected / respected / part of something

Jobs that are “interesting” often score high on the first two, and sometimes the third. Across studies, satisfaction of these needs is consistently associated with:
- higher job satisfaction
- better performance
- more engagement
- lower burnout

So if your current role is more intellectually alive, more agentic, and more growth-producing, the literature would predict that this matters a lot.

---

## 2) How does this compare to salary?

This is one of the better-established findings: **pay matters less for job satisfaction than most people think**.

A commonly cited meta-analysis is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)** on pay and job satisfaction

The bottom line from that literature is that the correlation between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** is **positive but modest**. In plain English:
- people do like being paid more,
- but pay usually explains only a **small fraction** of why people are satisfied with their job.

That is especially relevant when the choice is **not between financial insecurity and security**, but between **two already well-compensated jobs**. At high income levels, the marginal well-being benefit of an additional salary bump tends to be smaller.

### Broader happiness research on income
For general well-being, the literature is slightly more complicated:

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** found that higher income strongly improves **life evaluation**, while emotional well-being rose up to a point in their data.
- **Killingsworth (2021)** found well-being continuing to rise with income, including above earlier suggested thresholds, though with nuances.
- The broad consensus now is not “money stops mattering,” but rather:
  - **money matters a lot at lower levels**
  - and **less per additional dollar** at higher levels

So for someone already at a very high compensation level, a “bit higher salary” usually would not be expected to dominate the effect of daily work quality.

---

## 3) Meaningful / engaging work tends to predict well-being quite strongly

There is also a “meaningful work” literature, which overlaps a lot with “interesting work.”

Across reviews and meta-analyses, **meaningful work** is reliably associated with:
- higher **job satisfaction**
- higher **work engagement**
- lower **turnover intentions**
- better **general well-being**

This doesn’t mean meaning is everything, but it does mean that if one job feels substantially more important, more aligned, or more intrinsically compelling, that is not a soft factor. It is a major predictor in the literature.

---

## 4) Intrinsic vs extrinsic motivation: important for your case

Your tradeoff sounds close to a classic distinction:

- **Intrinsic motives**: the work itself is interesting, absorbing, growthful
- **Extrinsic motives**: money, title, status, prestige

A large body of work in motivation psychology suggests that **intrinsic motivation** is more strongly tied to:
- sustained engagement
- persistence
- enjoyment
- high-quality performance
- long-term well-being

Extrinsic rewards are not bad. But if they displace intrinsic interest, well-being often suffers.

A useful generalization from the literature is:

> **People live the daily texture of their work much more often than they experience the abstract fact of having a higher salary or title.**

That is partly why daily job characteristics often predict happiness better than compensation differences among already advantaged workers.

---

## 5) What about taking the team-lead role?

This is the most nuanced part.

You mention that **leading a small team is something you always wanted to do**. That is important, because from a research perspective, leadership is not purely “extrinsic.” For many people it is intrinsically rewarding because it offers:

- greater autonomy
- a stronger sense of impact
- mentoring/generativity
- a larger scope of agency
- identity fulfillment (“this is a role I wanted”)

If that is true for you, the lead role may satisfy deep intrinsic needs even if the technical domain is less interesting.

### But management often comes with real costs
The literature on managers/leaders suggests they often get:
- more control
- more status
- sometimes more satisfaction

but also:
- more stress
- longer hours
- emotional labor
- responsibility without full control
- role conflict

So leadership is often good **if the person actually wants the work of management**, not just the idea of promotion.

A lot of people want “to lead” in the abstract but dislike:
- performance management
- coordination overhead
- hiring
- conflict resolution
- less time on hands-on technical work

Research-wise, this is more of a **person-role fit** question than a universal answer.

If the team-lead role fulfills a genuine aspiration, it could offset some of the “less interesting subject matter” cost. If the leadership part is mostly symbolic/status-oriented, it often won’t.

---

## 6) Do people regret choosing money/status over interesting work?

On **explicit regret**, there is less direct, clean evidence than on satisfaction. But several adjacent findings are relevant:

### a) People adapt to pay/status gains
Research on **hedonic adaptation** suggests that people often adjust relatively quickly to improved circumstances, especially when those improvements are abstract or become the new normal.

That means:
- the higher salary may feel very meaningful at first,
- but the daily experience of less interesting work may remain salient for much longer.

### b) Daily experience matters a lot
Experience-sampling and daily affect research shows that day-to-day activities strongly shape subjective well-being. Since work occupies a huge fraction of waking life, the **moment-to-moment quality of work** matters more than many “one-time” decision factors.

### c) Intrinsic-goal prioritization predicts better well-being
Across self-determination research, people who orient more toward **intrinsic goals** tend to have better well-being than people who primarily optimize around **extrinsic rewards**.

That doesn’t prove “everyone regrets taking more money,” but it does support the idea that people often undervalue interestingness/meaning relative to salary and status.

So the research-based answer is something like:

- **Happier?** Often yes: people in work that is more autonomous, interesting, meaningful, and growthful tend to be happier at work, and often better off overall.
- **Regret?** Less directly studied, but the surrounding evidence suggests people are likely to **underestimate the long-run cost of boring/misaligned work** and **overestimate the long-run benefit of salary/status gains**.

---

## 7) One major caveat: “interesting” is not always enough

Interesting work is not always better if it comes with:
- chronic overload
- low control
- toxic politics
- poor manager
- bad culture
- unstable expectations

This is where the **Job Demands–Resources** literature matters. High challenge can be energizing, but high demand without sufficient autonomy/support causes burnout.

So a more complete research-based framing is:

### Strong predictors of better work well-being:
- autonomy
- competence/growth
- meaning/significance
- good colleagues/manager
- psychological safety
- manageable workload
- value fit

### Moderate predictor:
- compensation, especially once already high

### Very person-dependent:
- promotion into management / leadership

---

## 8) If I had to compress the empirical takeaway

If two jobs are both excellent and both pay very well, the literature would generally predict that **longer-term well-being** depends more on:

1. **How much you enjoy the actual work**
2. **How much autonomy and growth it gives you**
3. **Whether the role fits your values and identity**
4. **Whether the leadership aspect is a genuine source of meaning for you**
5. **How sustainable the day-to-day life is**

more than on a modest salary increase by itself.

So, in direct response to your question:

> **Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier?**

**Generally, yes**, according to work-design, motivation, and meaningful-work research.

> **Or do they regret that?**

There is less direct regret evidence, but the broader literature suggests people often **do not regret intrinsic quality as much as they regret losing it**, because salary and status gains are easier to adapt to than daily uninspiring work.

---

## Representative sources to look up

If you want the actual papers/books, these are good starting points:

- **Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R.** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design
- **Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010)** — pay and job satisfaction
- **Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M.** — Self-Determination Theory
- **Gagné, M., & Deci, E. L. (2005)** — SDT and work motivation
- **Van den Broeck et al.** — basic psychological needs at work
- **Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)** — review of meaningful work
- **Allan et al.** — meaningful work and well-being meta-analytic/review work
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)** — income and well-being
- **Killingsworth (2021)** — income and experienced well-being

If you want, I can do one of two follow-ups:

1. give you a **more rigorous literature review with 10–15 concrete papers and findings**, or  
2. turn this into a **decision framework grounded in the research** rather than personal advice.
